questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the hydroboration-oxidation of 1-methylcyclopentene:

Which of the following correctly identifies the products formed in this reaction?
I.
II.
III.
IV.
V.
options: An equal mixture of II and IV
An equal mixture of I and II
An equal mixture of I and V
An equal mixture of IV and V
questions.solution:
Hydroboration-oxidation of 1-methylcyclopentene results in the anti-Markovnikov, syn addition of water across the double bond.

The $\text{OH}$ group attaches to the less substituted carbon (C2), while the $\text{H}$ atom attaches to the more substituted carbon (C1).

Because the addition is syn, the incoming $\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$ groups must add to the same face of the double bond.

If addition occurs from the top face, the added $\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$ are on wedges. The original $\text{CH}_3$ at C1 and $\text{H}$ at C2 are pushed to dashes. This yields structure IV, which is a trans isomer.

If addition occurs from the bottom face, the added $\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$ are on dashes. The original $\text{CH}_3$ at C1 and $\text{H}$ at C2 are pushed to wedges. This yields structure II, which is the enantiomer of IV and also a trans isomer.

Both faces of the planar alkene are equally likely to be attacked, resulting in an equal mixture of enantiomers II and IV.

Answer: An equal mixture of II and IV

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:
$\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2} \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{NaHSO}_{3}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{OsO}_{4}} \text{(A)} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KHSO}_{4}, \Delta} \text{(B)}$
The major product (B) is:
options: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{COOH}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}$
questions.solution:
The starting material is allyl alcohol, $\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$.

Reaction with $\mathrm{OsO}_{4}$ followed by $\mathrm{NaHSO}_{3}$ causes syn-dihydroxylation of the carbon-carbon double bond.

This yields glycerol, $\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$ as product (A).

Heating glycerol with a dehydrating agent like $\mathrm{KHSO}_{4}$ results in the elimination of two molecules of water.

This dehydration process yields acrolein, $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}$ as the major product (B).

Answer: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reaction conditions for the transformations A, B, and C shown in the following scheme:
options: A: Oxymercuration-demercuration; B: Acid-catalysed hydration; C: Hydroboration-oxidation
A: Acid-catalysed hydration; B: Hydroboration-oxidation; C: Oxymercuration-demercuration
A: Hydroboration-oxidation; B: Oxymercuration-demercuration; C: Acid-catalysed hydration
A: Oxymercuration-demercuration; B: Hydroboration-oxidation; C: Acid-catalysed hydration
questions.solution:
The starting material is vinylcyclohexane.

Transformation A yields 2-cyclohexylethanol. This is an anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond, which is achieved by hydroboration-oxidation.

Transformation B yields 1-cyclohexylethanol. This is a Markovnikov addition of water without any skeletal rearrangement, which is achieved by oxymercuration-demercuration.

Transformation C yields 1-ethylcyclohexanol. This product is formed via a carbocation rearrangement, characteristic of acid-catalysed hydration. Protonation of the alkene forms a secondary carbocation at the carbon adjacent to the ring. A $1,2$-hydride shift from the tertiary ring carbon generates a more stable tertiary carbocation, which is then attacked by water to form the tertiary alcohol.

Thus, A is Hydroboration-oxidation, B is Oxymercuration-demercuration, and C is Acid-catalysed hydration.

Answer: A: Hydroboration-oxidation; B: Oxymercuration-demercuration; C: Acid-catalysed hydration

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{P}$ obtained in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed dehydration of an alcohol, which proceeds via an E1 mechanism involving a carbocation intermediate.

Step 1: Protonation of the hydroxyl group followed by the loss of a water molecule generates a secondary carbocation at C1.

Step 2: The adjacent carbon (C2) is a quaternary carbon with two methyl groups. To achieve greater stability, a 1,2-methyl shift occurs from C2 to C1. This rearrangement converts the secondary carbocation into a more stable tertiary carbocation at C2.

Step 3: The rearranged tertiary carbocation can now undergo elimination of a proton ($H^+$) from an adjacent carbon (either C1 or C3) to form a double bond.
- Elimination of a proton from C3 yields a trisubstituted alkene (double bond between C2 and C3).
- Elimination of a proton from C1 yields a tetrasubstituted alkene (double bond between C1 and C2).

According to Zaitsev's rule, the more substituted alkene is thermodynamically more stable and is formed as the major product. Therefore, the elimination predominantly occurs from C1 to give the tetrasubstituted alkene, which is 1,2,4-trimethylcyclopent-1-ene.

Comparing this with the given options, Option (B) correctly represents this tetrasubstituted alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A hydrocarbon $\left(\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{8}\right)$ on reaction with m-chloroperbenzoic acid (MCPBA) gives compound (X). Compound (X) on reaction with aqueous KOH gives (Y), which on treatment with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ forms 2-methylpropanal. The hydrocarbon is:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
The reaction of a hydrocarbon with $m$-chloroperbenzoic acid (MCPBA) indicates the presence of an alkene, which undergoes epoxidation to form an epoxide (X).

The epoxide (X) reacts with aqueous KOH to undergo ring opening, forming a vicinal diol (Y).

Treatment of the vicinal diol (Y) with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ results in a pinacol-pinacolone rearrangement to form 2-methylpropanal, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CHO}$.

In the pinacol rearrangement, the most stable carbocation is formed first. For 2-methylpropane-1,2-diol, protonation and loss of water from the tertiary hydroxyl group yields a stable tertiary carbocation:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{OH})(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

A hydride shift from the adjacent carbon forms a resonance-stabilized oxocarbenium ion, which upon deprotonation gives 2-methylpropanal:

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydride shift}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{O}^{+}H \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})-\mathrm{CHO}$

Thus, the diol (Y) is 2-methylpropane-1,2-diol.

The epoxide (X) that hydrolyzes to this diol is 2,2-dimethyloxirane.

The alkene that reacts with MCPBA to form 2,2-dimethyloxirane is 2-methylpropene, $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Compound A is treated with a large excess of $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{MgBr}$. The resulting product is then exposed to $\mathrm{POCl}_3$ in pyridine to give compound B (shown below) as one of the products.

Which of the following compounds is A?
questions.solution:
Compound B has three exocyclic double bonds on a cyclohexane ring: one $=CH_2$ group and two $=C(CH_3)_2$ groups.

These double bonds are formed by the dehydration of tertiary alcohols using $POCl_3$ in pyridine.

The exocyclic $=CH_2$ group is formed from a cyclic tertiary alcohol containing a methyl group, which is produced by the nucleophilic addition of $CH_3MgBr$ to a cyclic ketone.

The exocyclic $=C(CH_3)_2$ groups are formed from $-C(OH)(CH_3)_2$ groups. These tertiary alcohols are produced by the reaction of $CH_3MgBr$ with a methyl ketone ($-COCH_3$) using one equivalent, or with an ester ($-COOCH_3$) using two equivalents.

Therefore, the starting material A must contain one cyclic ketone group and two groups that are either methyl ketones or esters. Evaluating the given choices, option (1) has exactly one cyclic ketone ($=O$), one methyl ester ($-COOCH_3$), and one methyl ketone ($-COCH_3$) at the 1, 3, and 5 positions respectively.

Treatment of the compound in option (1) with excess $CH_3MgBr$ yields the corresponding triol. Subsequent dehydration of this triol with $POCl_3$ in pyridine gives Compound B.

Answer :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagent sequences is most appropriate for the given transformation?
options: (i) $\mathrm{BH}_{3}$ (ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{NaOH}$
(i) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ (ii) $\mathrm{NaOH}$
$\mathrm{HBr}$ / peroxides
(i) aq. $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ (ii) $\mathrm{Na}$ (iii) $\mathrm{NaI}$ / acetone
questions.solution:
The transformation requires the conversion of $5$-chloro-$1$-pentene to tetrahydropyran, a $6$-membered cyclic ether.

To form this $6$-membered ring, an oxygen atom must be introduced at the terminal carbon of the alkene, which can then undergo an intramolecular nucleophilic substitution to displace the chlorine atom.

Hydroboration-oxidation using (i) $\mathrm{BH}_{3}$ and (ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{NaOH}$ results in the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond, yielding $5$-chloro-$1$-pentanol:
$\mathrm{Cl-(CH_{2})_{3}-CH=CH_{2}} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{BH}_{3}, \text{ then } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2}/\mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{Cl-(CH_{2})_{5}-OH}$

In the basic medium provided by $\mathrm{NaOH}$, the primary alcohol is deprotonated to form an alkoxide ion:
$\mathrm{Cl-(CH_{2})_{5}-OH} + \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Cl-(CH_{2})_{5}-O^{-}} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

The alkoxide ion then undergoes a rapid intramolecular $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ reaction, attacking the carbon bearing the chlorine atom and displacing the chloride ion to form the $6$-membered tetrahydropyran ring.

Other options would not yield the desired product:
Acid-catalyzed hydration would give a Markovnikov product (secondary alcohol), leading to a $5$-membered ring ($2$-methyltetrahydrofuran) upon cyclization.
$\mathrm{HBr}$ / peroxides would yield $1$-bromo-$5$-chloropentane, introducing no oxygen.

Answer: (i) $\mathrm{BH}_{3}$ (ii) $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{NaOH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following two-step reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves two steps:

Step 1: The starting material, an alcohol with the -OH group on a wedge, reacts with $p$-toluenesulfonyl chloride (TsCl) in the presence of pyridine. This reaction converts the poor leaving group (-OH) into a good leaving group, a tosylate (-OTs). Because the C-O bond is not broken during this step, the reaction proceeds with retention of configuration. Thus, the intermediate formed will have the -OTs group on a wedge.

Step 2: The tosylate intermediate is then treated with sodium cyanide (NaCN) in dimethylformamide (DMF). DMF is a polar aprotic solvent, which strongly favors an $S_N2$ mechanism. In an $S_N2$ reaction, the nucleophile ($CN^-$) attacks the electrophilic carbon from the side opposite to the leaving group (backside attack).

Since the -OTs leaving group is positioned on a wedge (pointing towards the viewer), the incoming $CN^-$ nucleophile must attack from the back, which corresponds to a dash (pointing away from the viewer). This results in an inversion of configuration at the chiral center.

Therefore, the final product will have the -CN group on a dash. This matches the structure shown in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the starting material (?) in the following reaction sequence:

? $\xrightarrow[\text{2. } \mathrm{O}_3\text{, then } \mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COOH}]{\text{1. } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4\text{, heat}}$ $\mathrm{OHC}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence consists of two steps: dehydration of an alcohol using $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ and heat to form an alkene, followed by reductive ozonolysis of the alkene using $\mathrm{O}_3$ and $\mathrm{Zn}/\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COOH}$ to form carbonyl compounds.

The final product is hexanedial, $\mathrm{OHC}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO}$, which is a six-carbon dialdehyde.

Working backwards, the alkene that yields hexanedial upon reductive ozonolysis must be a six-membered cyclic alkene, which is cyclohexene.

The starting material that undergoes dehydration to form cyclohexene is cyclohexanol.

For comparison, 1-methylcyclopentanol undergoes dehydration to give 1-methylcyclopentene. Reductive ozonolysis of 1-methylcyclopentene yields 5-oxohexanal ($\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CHO}$), which contains a ketone group rather than two aldehyde groups. Methylcyclopentane and 1-bromo-1-methylcyclopentane do not undergo dehydration under these conditions.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following substrates yields a rearranged product upon acid-catalyzed hydration?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\ddot{\mathrm{S}}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Acid-catalyzed hydration of alkenes proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate, which may undergo rearrangement (such as a hydride or alkyl shift) if a more stable carbocation can be formed.

Let us analyze the intermediate formed for each substrate:

For (A): Protonation of the double bond yields $Ph_2C^{+}-CH_2-C(CH_3)_3$. This is a tertiary carbocation that is highly resonance-stabilized by two phenyl groups. Since it is already exceptionally stable, it does not undergo any rearrangement.

For (B): Protonation yields $CH_3-\ddot{S}-CH_2-C^{+}H-CH_3$. The lone pair on the sulfur atom at the $\beta$-position participates in neighboring group participation (NGP) to form a stable cyclic episulfonium ion. This prevents any hydride shift, and subsequent attack by water yields the unrearranged product.

For (C): The substrate is 3-methylbut-1-ene. Protonation of the double bond yields a secondary carbocation: $CH_3-CH(CH_3)-C^{+}H-CH_3$. This secondary carbocation is adjacent to a tertiary carbon, so it undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift to form a more stable tertiary carbocation: $CH_3-C^{+}(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3$. Attack by water on this rearranged carbocation yields 2-methylbutan-2-ol, which is a rearranged product.

For (D): Protonation of propene yields the isopropyl cation, $CH_3-C^{+}H-CH_3$. This is a secondary carbocation and cannot rearrange to a more stable carbocation, so no rearrangement occurs.

Therefore, only the substrate in option (C) yields a rearranged product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:

Identify the structure of the major product $\mathrm{Q}$.
questions.solution:
The reaction of cyclobutylmethanol with $\mathrm{H_2SO_4}$ under heating is an acid-catalyzed dehydration.

Protonation of the hydroxyl group and subsequent loss of a water molecule generates a primary cyclobutylmethyl carbocation.

Due to the high angle strain in the four-membered ring, a 1,2-alkyl shift occurs, resulting in ring expansion to form a more stable secondary cyclopentyl carbocation (cyclobutylmethyl carbocation $\rightarrow$ cyclopentyl carbocation).

Loss of a proton from the cyclopentyl carbocation yields cyclopentene as the major product $\mathrm{P}$.

Next, cyclopentene reacts with N-bromosuccinimide (NBS), which is a specific reagent for allylic bromination.

NBS selectively substitutes a bromine atom for a hydrogen atom at the allylic position (the carbon atom adjacent to the carbon-carbon double bond) via a free radical mechanism.

This bromination of cyclopentene yields 3-bromocyclopentene as the major product $\mathrm{Q}$.

Among the given options, structure (C) correctly represents 3-bromocyclopentene, with the bromine atom at the allylic position.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product [Z] in the following reaction sequence:
$CH_3-\overset{\displaystyle CH_3}{\overset{|}{C}}=CH_2 \xrightarrow{\displaystyle MCPBA / CH_2Cl_2} [X] \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \Large H_2O^{\oplus}]{(i)\ \Large H^{\oplus}} [Y] \xrightarrow{\displaystyle Conc.\ H^{\oplus}} [Z]$

The major product (Z) can be
questions.solution:
Reaction with MCPBA converts the alkene $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}=\text{CH}_2$ into an epoxide, 2,2-dimethyloxirane [X].

The epoxide undergoes acid-catalyzed ring opening with $\text{H}_2^{18}\text{O}$. Protonation of the epoxide oxygen is followed by nucleophilic attack of $\text{H}_2^{18}\text{O}$ at the more substituted tertiary carbon, because the transition state possesses a highly stable tertiary carbocation character. This produces 2-methylpropane-1,2-diol [Y] with the heavy oxygen isotope at the tertiary position: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}(^{18}\text{OH})-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$.

Treatment of this diol with concentrated acid initiates a pinacol-type rearrangement. Protonation of the tertiary hydroxyl group and subsequent loss of water ($\text{H}_2^{18}\text{O}$) generates a stable tertiary carbocation: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C}^+-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$. The alternative formation of a primary carbocation is energetically unfavorable.

A 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent carbon to the carbocation center forms a resonance-stabilized oxonium ion: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CH}-\text{CH}=\text{O}^+\text{H}$.

Deprotonation yields the final product [Z], 2-methylpropanal: $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CH}-\text{CHO}$. Since the labeled oxygen was lost as water during the rearrangement, the final product contains no $^{18}\text{O}$ isotope.

Answer :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following alcohols is most reactive towards aqueous $\mathrm{HBr}$?
options: 1-Phenyl-2-propanol
1-Phenyl-1-propanol
3-Phenyl-1-propanol
2-Phenyl-1-propanol
questions.solution:
The reaction of an alcohol with aqueous $\text{HBr}$ proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate.

The reactivity of the alcohol depends directly on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed after protonation and loss of a water molecule.

For 1-phenyl-2-propanol, the intermediate is a secondary carbocation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}^{+}\text{H}-\text{CH}_3$.

For 1-phenyl-1-propanol, the intermediate is a secondary benzylic carbocation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{C}^{+}\text{H}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_3$. This carbocation is highly stabilized by resonance with the phenyl ring.

For 3-phenyl-1-propanol, the intermediate is a primary carbocation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}^{+}\text{H}_2$.

For 2-phenyl-1-propanol, the intermediate is a primary carbocation: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{C}^{+}\text{H}_2$.

Since the secondary benzylic carbocation is the most stable among the given options, 1-phenyl-1-propanol is the most reactive towards aqueous $\text{HBr}$.

Answer : 1-Phenyl-1-propanol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction sequence:

If $x$ and $y$ represent the total number of products (including stereoisomers) formed in the respective steps, what are the values of $x$ and $y$?
options: 3 and 5
2 and 4
2 and 3
3 and 6
questions.solution:
Dehydration of 2-pentanol with concentrated $H_2SO_4$ and heat proceeds via an E1 mechanism to form alkenes.

The intermediate is a secondary carbocation at C-2: $CH_3-\overset{+}{C}H-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3$.

Elimination of a proton from C-1 yields 1-pentene (no stereoisomers).

Elimination of a proton from C-3 yields 2-pentene, which exhibits geometrical isomerism and exists as cis-2-pentene and trans-2-pentene.

Thus, the total number of alkene products is $x = 1 + 2 = 3$.

Bromination of these alkenes with $Br_2/CCl_4$ proceeds via anti-addition:

1. Addition to 1-pentene yields 1,2-dibromopentane. It has one chiral center at C-2, forming a pair of enantiomers (2 products).

2. Addition to 2-pentene yields 2,3-dibromopentane. It has two chiral centers at C-2 and C-3. Since the molecule is unsymmetrical (methyl and ethyl groups at the ends), it forms $2^2 = 4$ distinct stereoisomers. Specifically, cis-2-pentene gives a racemic mixture (threo pair, 2 products), and trans-2-pentene gives another racemic mixture (erythro pair, 2 products).

Thus, the total number of brominated products is $y = 2 + 4 = 6$.

Therefore, $x = 3$ and $y = 6$.

Answer: 3 and 6

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed upon the hydroboration-oxidation of 1-methylcyclopentene.
questions.solution:
Hydroboration-oxidation of an alkene results in the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond.

In 1-methylcyclopentene, the double bond is between C1 (bearing the methyl group) and C2. According to anti-Markovnikov's rule, the $OH$ group attaches to the less sterically hindered, less substituted carbon (C2), and the $H$ atom attaches to the more substituted carbon (C1).

The reaction proceeds via a syn-addition mechanism, meaning the $H$ atom and the $OH$ group add to the same face of the double bond.

Since the added $H$ and $OH$ are cis to each other, the pre-existing methyl group at C1 must be trans to the newly added $OH$ group at C2.

Therefore, the major product is trans-2-methylcyclopentanol, where the methyl and $OH$ groups are on opposite faces of the ring (one wedged and one dashed).

Option (C) correctly shows trans-2-methylcyclopentanol with the methyl group on a wedge and the $OH$ group on a dash.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their rate of dehydration when treated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$.

(P)
(Q)
(R)
(S)
options: R > Q > P > S
R > Q > S > P
Q > P > R > S
P > Q > R > S
questions.solution:
The rate of dehydration of alcohols when treated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ depends on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed during the rate-determining step.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed by the protonation and subsequent loss of water from each compound:

1. Compound P: Loss of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group forms a primary ($1^{\circ}$) allylic carbocation. This carbocation is resonance stabilized, with the positive charge delocalizing to a secondary carbon on the ring. Furthermore, its dehydration leads to a highly stable conjugated diene.

2. Compound Q: Loss of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group forms a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) allylic carbocation. This is more stable than a primary allylic carbocation due to the additional electron-donating alkyl group.

3. Compound R: Loss of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group forms a tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) allylic carbocation. This is the most stable carbocation among the given options due to both extensive hyperconjugation (from the methyl group and ring carbons) and resonance stabilization.

4. Compound S: Loss of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group forms a secondary ($2^{\circ}$) alkyl carbocation. It lacks resonance stabilization and is only stabilized by hyperconjugation, making it the least stable among the four.

The general order of stability for these carbocations is:
$3^{\circ}$ allylic (R) > $2^{\circ}$ allylic (Q) > $1^{\circ}$ allylic (P) > $2^{\circ}$ alkyl (S)

Since the rate of dehydration is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation, the decreasing order of the rate of dehydration is R > Q > P > S.

Answer: R > Q > P > S

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{X}$ in the following reaction:
options: Reaction will not occur
questions.solution:
The given reagent, $\text{OsO}_4$, is used for the syn-dihydroxylation of alkenes. It acts as an electrophilic reagent and preferentially attacks more electron-rich double bonds.

The reactant molecule contains two double bonds:
1. An unsubstituted double bond.
2. A double bond substituted with two ester ($-\text{COOCH}_3$) groups.

The ester groups are strongly electron-withdrawing due to their $-M$ and $-I$ effects. This significantly reduces the electron density of the double bond they are attached to, making it less reactive towards electrophilic attack.

Consequently, the unsubstituted double bond is relatively more electron-rich and more reactive. Since only 1 equivalent of $\text{OsO}_4$ is provided, the dihydroxylation will selectively occur at the unsubstituted double bond.

This results in the formation of the product where the unsubstituted double bond is converted into a vicinal diol, while the substituted double bond remains intact. This matches the structure given in option (A).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major end products $\mathrm{A}$, $\mathrm{B}$, and $\mathrm{C}$ for the following reactions:

$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{D}^{\oplus}} \mathrm{A}$

$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{H}^{\oplus}} \mathrm{B}$

$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{D}^{\oplus}} \mathrm{C}$
options:
$\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$,
$\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_3$,
$\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$
in all cases
in all cases
in all cases
questions.solution:
The acid-catalyzed hydration of an alkene proceeds via an electrophilic addition mechanism following Markovnikov's rule.

Step 1: The electrophile ($\mathrm{H}^{\oplus}$ or $\mathrm{D}^{\oplus}$) attacks the double bond to form the more stable secondary carbocation.

Step 2: The nucleophile ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ or $\mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O}$) attacks the carbocation, followed by deprotonation or dedeuteration to give the final alcohol.

For reaction 1:
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 + \mathrm{D}^{\oplus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D} + \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$ (Product A)

For reaction 2:
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 + \mathrm{H}^{\oplus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_3$
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_3 + \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_3$ (Product B)

For reaction 3:
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2 + \mathrm{D}^{\oplus} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$
$\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}^{\oplus}\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D} + \mathrm{D}_2\mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$ (Product C)

Answer: $\mathrm{A} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$,
$\mathrm{B} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_3$,
$\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OD})\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the products $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ in the following reaction sequence:
$\mathrm{B} \underset{\mathrm{H}^{+}}{\stackrel{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}}{\longleftarrow}} \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{A}$
options: $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ both are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ both are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{A}$ is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$, while $\mathrm{B}$ is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ both are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ (i.e., there is no reaction)
questions.solution:
Tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation under neutral or alkaline conditions. Therefore, when treated with alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ ($\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$), no reaction occurs and product $\mathrm{A}$ is the unreacted starting material, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$.

Under acidic conditions ($\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$), tertiary alcohols undergo dehydration to form alkenes. Thus, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ dehydrates to give isobutylene, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$.

The resulting alkene can further undergo oxidative cleavage by the strong oxidizing agent (acidic $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$) to yield a ketone (acetone, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$) and carbon dioxide.

Therefore, product $\mathrm{B}$ is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{A}$ is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$, while $\mathrm{B}$ is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product obtained when the given alcohol is treated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
options: All the three will be formed in equal amounts
questions.solution:
The given alcohol is 1-phenyl-3-methylbutan-2-ol.

Upon treatment with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the hydroxyl group is protonated and leaves as a water molecule, forming a secondary carbocation:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH})-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$

This secondary carbocation can undergo a 1,2-hydride shift to form a more stable carbocation. There are two possible shifts.

A 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent benzylic $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-$ group forms a secondary benzylic carbocation, which is highly stabilized by resonance with the phenyl ring:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$

Alternatively, a 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent $-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$ group forms a tertiary carbocation:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$

Dehydration of alcohols is under thermodynamic control, meaning the most stable alkene will be the major product.

Elimination of a proton from the benzylic carbocation yields a conjugated alkene:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}^{+}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$

Elimination of a proton from the tertiary carbocation yields an isolated alkene:

$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{C}^{+}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$

The alkene $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$ is conjugated with the benzene ring, making it thermodynamically more stable than the isolated alkene $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$.

Therefore, the major product is 1-phenyl-3-methylbut-1-ene, which matches the structure in option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the role of $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}$ in the following reaction:
options: Base
Nucleophile
Catalyst
Leaving group
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed hydration of an alkene (methylenecyclohexane) to form an alcohol (1-methylcyclohexanol).

The mechanism involves the following steps:
1. The alkene double bond attacks the electrophilic proton from $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$, forming a stable tertiary carbocation intermediate. This step consumes $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$ and produces $\mathrm{H_2O}$.
2. A water molecule acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbocation, forming an oxonium ion intermediate.
3. Another water molecule removes a proton from the oxonium ion to yield the final neutral alcohol product, regenerating $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$ in the process.

Since $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$ is consumed in the first step and regenerated in the final step without being a part of the overall stoichiometry, it acts as a catalyst.

Answer: Catalyst

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product $\mathrm{X}$ in the following reaction sequence:


The compound X is
options: Both the compounds shown in Option A and Option B
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the hydroboration-oxidation of 1-methylcyclohexene.

The reagents $\mathrm{B_2H_6}$ followed by $\mathrm{H_2O_2} / \mathrm{OH^-}$ lead to the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond.

The $\mathrm{OH}$ group attaches to the less substituted carbon (C2), and the $\mathrm{H}$ atom attaches to the more substituted carbon (C1).

This addition is stereospecifically syn, meaning both $\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{OH}$ add to the same face of the alkene.

Assuming addition from the bottom face, the added $\mathrm{H}$ at C1 and the added $\mathrm{OH}$ at C2 will both point downwards. As a result, the $\mathrm{CH_3}$ group at C1 is pushed upwards.

This yields trans-2-methylcyclohexanol, where the $\mathrm{CH_3}$ group (up) and the $\mathrm{OH}$ group (down) are trans to each other.

The structure in Option (A) correctly depicts this syn addition product, showing the added $\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{OH}$ groups on the same side (downwards) and the $\mathrm{CH_3}$ and $\mathrm{OH}$ groups trans to each other.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product $[\mathrm{X}]$ in the following reaction:

$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2 / \mathrm{NaOH}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{BH}_3} [\mathrm{X}]$
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the hydroboration-oxidation of an alkene.

The reagents used are (i) $\mathrm{BH}_3$ and (ii) $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2 / \mathrm{NaOH}$. This reaction sequence leads to the anti-Markovnikov addition of water ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) across the carbon-carbon double bond.

In the reactant, 2-methylpropene ($(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2$), the terminal carbon is less substituted (less sterically hindered) than the internal carbon.

During hydroboration, the boron atom adds to the less hindered terminal carbon, and hydrogen adds to the more substituted internal carbon.

Subsequent oxidation replaces the boron atom with a hydroxyl ($-\mathrm{OH}$) group.

Therefore, the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group attaches to the terminal carbon, yielding 2-methylpropan-1-ol as the major product.

Reaction:
$(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2 / \mathrm{NaOH}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{BH}_3} (\mathrm{CH}_3)_2\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_2\mathrm{OH}$

This matches the structure given in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reactions yields a cis-diol as the major product?
options: $\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{KMnO}_{4}}$
$\xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{3}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{OsO}_{4}}$
questions.solution:
To determine which reaction yields a cis-diol, we need to analyze the stereochemistry of the addition reactions for each given reagent:

1. Reagents and their stereochemical outcomes:
- $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$ (cold, dilute) performs syn-hydroxylation.
- $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{HCOOH}$ generates performic acid in situ, which forms an epoxide that is subsequently opened by water, resulting in anti-hydroxylation.
- $\mathrm{OsO}_{4}$ followed by $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ performs syn-hydroxylation.

2. Analyzing the options:
- Option (A): The reactant is an acyclic cis-alkene. Syn-hydroxylation yields a meso-diol. The terms cis and trans are generally not used for acyclic diols.
- Option (B): The reactant is cyclopentene (a cyclic alkene). Anti-hydroxylation by $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{HCOOH}$ yields trans-1,2-cyclopentanediol.
- Option (C): The reactant is 1-ethyl-2-methylcyclopentene (a cyclic alkene). Syn-hydroxylation by $\mathrm{OsO}_{4} / \mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ adds two $-\mathrm{OH}$ groups to the same face of the ring, yielding a cis-diol (specifically, cis-1-ethyl-2-methylcyclopentane-1,2-diol).
- Option (D): The reactant is an acyclic trans-alkene. Syn-hydroxylation yields a racemic mixture of enantiomers (a dl-pair).

Therefore, the reaction in Option (C) is the one that yields a cis-diol as the major product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 1-Propanol can be prepared from propene using which of the following reagents?
options: $\mathrm{Hg}(\mathrm{OAc})_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ followed by $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$
All of these
$\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{6}-\mathrm{THF}$ followed by $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
questions.solution:
Propene is $\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2}$.

The preparation of 1-propanol ($\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-OH}$) from propene requires the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond.

Hydroboration-oxidation of alkenes results in the anti-Markovnikov addition of water. The reagents used for this process are $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{6}$ in THF followed by oxidation with $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2}$ in an alkaline medium ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$).

Oxymercuration-demercuration ($\mathrm{Hg}(\mathrm{OAc})_{2} / \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ followed by $\mathrm{NaBH}_{4}$) and acid-catalyzed hydration ($\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$) both follow Markovnikov's rule, which would yield 2-propanol instead of 1-propanol.

Answer: $\mathrm{B}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{6}-\mathrm{THF}$ followed by $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The decreasing order of reactivity of the following alcohols towards esterification is:
(i) Methyl alcohol
(ii) Isopropyl alcohol
(iii) Tertiary butyl alcohol
(iv) Ethyl alcohol
options: (i) > (iv) > (iii) > (ii)
(iv) > (iii) > (ii) > (i)
(i) > (iv) > (ii) > (iii)
(i) > (ii) > (iii) > (iv)
questions.solution:
Esterification is a nucleophilic acyl substitution reaction where the alcohol acts as a nucleophile.

The reactivity of an alcohol towards esterification depends on the steric hindrance around the hydroxyl group. As the size and branching of the alkyl group increase, steric hindrance increases, which decreases the rate of esterification.

The general order of reactivity of alcohols towards esterification is:
Methyl alcohol > Primary ($1^{\circ}$) alcohol > Secondary ($2^{\circ}$) alcohol > Tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) alcohol.

Classifying the given alcohols:
(i) Methyl alcohol: $CH_3OH$ (least sterically hindered)
(iv) Ethyl alcohol: $CH_3CH_2OH$ ($1^{\circ}$ alcohol)
(ii) Isopropyl alcohol: $(CH_3)_2CHOH$ ($2^{\circ}$ alcohol)
(iii) Tertiary butyl alcohol: $(CH_3)_3COH$ ($3^{\circ}$ alcohol, most sterically hindered)

Therefore, the decreasing order of reactivity is (i) > (iv) > (ii) > (iii).

Answer: (i) > (iv) > (ii) > (iii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ethers cannot be prepared by Williamson ether synthesis?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{PhOCH}_{3}$
$\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
Williamson ether synthesis involves an $S_{N}2$ reaction between an alkoxide or aroxide ion and an alkyl halide. For the $S_{N}2$ reaction to proceed successfully, the alkyl halide must have the halogen attached to an $sp^{3}$ hybridized carbon (preferably primary or methyl).

In option (A), $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ can be prepared by reacting sodium methoxide with allyl halide, or sodium allyloxide with methyl halide.

In option (B), $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ has oxygen bonded to two $sp^{2}$ hybridized vinylic carbons. Its synthesis would require a vinyl halide, which does not undergo $S_{N}2$ reactions due to the partial double bond character of the C-X bond and the instability of the transition state. Thus, it cannot be prepared by Williamson ether synthesis.

In option (C), $\mathrm{PhOCH}_{3}$ can be prepared by reacting sodium phenoxide with methyl halide.

In option (D), $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}$ can be prepared by reacting sodium tert-butoxide with 1-halopropane (a primary alkyl halide).

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the role of $\mathrm{H}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{+}$ in the following reaction:
options: Nucleophile
Base
Catalyst
Leaving group
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed hydration of an alkene (methylenecyclohexane) to form an alcohol (1-methylcyclohexanol).

The mechanism involves the following steps:
1. Protonation of the alkene: The double bond attacks the electrophilic proton from $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$, forming a stable tertiary carbocation (1-methylcyclohexyl cation) and a water molecule.
2. Nucleophilic attack: A water molecule acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbocation, forming a protonated alcohol (oxonium ion).
3. Deprotonation: Another water molecule removes a proton from the oxonium ion to yield the final neutral alcohol product, regenerating $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$.

Since $\mathrm{H_3O^+}$ is consumed in the first step and regenerated in the final step without being consumed in the overall stoichiometry, it acts as a catalyst.

Answer: Catalyst

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the correct decreasing order of reactivity for the following alcohols towards substitution with a mixture of $\mathrm{NaBr}$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$:

(I)
(II)
(III)
(IV)
options: (I) > (II) > (III) > (IV)
(IV) > (III) > (II) > (I)
(III) > (I) > (II) > (IV)
(IV) > (II) > (I) > (III)
questions.solution:
The reaction of alcohols with a mixture of $\mathrm{NaBr}$ and $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{SO}_4$ generates $\mathrm{HBr}$, which acts as the reagent for the nucleophilic substitution of the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group by $-\mathrm{Br}$.

Since the substrates are benzylic alcohols, the reaction proceeds primarily via an $S_N1$ mechanism. The rate-determining step is the formation of a benzylic carbocation intermediate after the protonation of the alcohol and subsequent loss of a water molecule.

The reactivity of the alcohols is directly proportional to the stability of the intermediate carbocation. The stability of the carbocation is influenced by the electronic effects of the substituents present on the benzene ring:

In (IV), the $-\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2$ group exhibits a strong +R (resonance) effect, which highly stabilizes the positive charge on the benzylic carbon.

In (II), the $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group exhibits +I (inductive) and +H (hyperconjugation) effects, which also stabilize the carbocation, but to a lesser extent than the +R effect of $-\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2$.

In (I), there is no substituent on the ring, serving as a reference.

In (III), the $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group exhibits strong -R and -I effects, which highly destabilize the carbocation.

Therefore, the decreasing order of stability of the intermediate carbocations is (IV) > (II) > (I) > (III).

Consequently, the decreasing order of reactivity of the given alcohols is (IV) > (II) > (I) > (III).

Answer: (IV) > (II) > (I) > (III)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction:
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{HI} / \Delta} [\mathrm{X}] + [\mathrm{Y}]$
The products $[\mathrm{X}]$ and $[\mathrm{Y}]$ are:
options: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{I}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{I}$
$\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the cleavage of an alkyl aryl ether by hydroiodic acid ($\mathrm{HI}$).

First, the ether oxygen is protonated by $\mathrm{HI}$ to form an oxonium ion: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{H})-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$.

The $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}$ bond has partial double bond character due to the resonance of the lone pair of oxygen with the benzene ring, making it stronger and difficult to break. Therefore, the nucleophile $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ attacks the less sterically hindered $\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ carbon of the ethyl group via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism.

This results in the cleavage of the $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ bond, yielding phenol ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$) and ethyl iodide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{I}$) as the final products.

Answer: $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{I}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the product(s) formed in the following reaction:
options: Both A and B
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 2-phenylethyl tosylate with an isotopic label ($^{14}C$) at the $\alpha$-carbon: $Ph-CH_2-^{14}CH_2-OTs$.

The reagent is sodium methoxide ($CH_3ONa$) in methanol ($CH_3OH$), which provides the strong nucleophile $CH_3O^-$.

For a primary substrate reacting with a strong nucleophile, the bimolecular nucleophilic substitution ($S_N2$) mechanism is highly favored over neighboring group participation (NGP) by the phenyl group.

In the $S_N2$ mechanism, the methoxide ion directly attacks the $\alpha$-carbon, displacing the tosylate leaving group without any rearrangement.

Therefore, the nucleophile attaches to the $^{14}C$-labeled carbon, yielding the unrearranged product: $Ph-CH_2-^{14}CH_2-OCH_3$.

This corresponds to the structure shown in the first option.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The conversion of ethanol to propanenitrile is best achieved by:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{KCN} \xrightarrow{\text{TsCl / Pyridine, } \Delta}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{HCN} \xrightarrow{\Delta}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) KCN}]{\text{(i) TsCl / Pyridine}}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CN} \xrightarrow{\Delta}$
questions.solution:
Ethanol ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$) cannot undergo nucleophilic substitution directly with $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ because the hydroxide ion ($\mathrm{OH}^{-}$) is a poor leaving group.

First, ethanol is reacted with $p$-toluenesulfonyl chloride (TsCl) in the presence of pyridine to convert the poor leaving group ($\mathrm{-OH}$) into an excellent leaving group, tosylate ($\mathrm{-OTs}$).

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{TsCl / Pyridine}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OTs}$

In the second step, the ethyl tosylate is treated with potassium cyanide ($\mathrm{KCN}$). The cyanide ion ($\mathrm{CN}^{-}$) acts as a strong nucleophile and displaces the tosylate group via an $\mathrm{S_{N}2}$ mechanism to yield propanenitrile.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OTs} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KCN}} \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CN} + \mathrm{KOTs}$

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH} \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) KCN}]{\text{(i) TsCl / Pyridine}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$
questions.solution:
The given reactant is allyl vinyl ether, $\mathrm{CH_2=CH-O-CH_2-CH=CH_2}$.

When heated to around $200^\circ\mathrm{C}$, allyl vinyl ethers undergo a [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement known as the Claisen rearrangement.

The reaction proceeds via a six-membered cyclic transition state. The steps involved are:
1. The $\mathrm{C-O}$ bond of the allyl group breaks.
2. A new $\mathrm{C-C}$ bond forms between the terminal carbon of the vinyl group and the terminal carbon of the allyl group.
3. The double bonds shift, resulting in the formation of a carbonyl group ($\mathrm{C=O}$) and a new $\mathrm{C=C}$ double bond.

Tracing the atoms during the rearrangement:
$\mathrm{CH_2(3)=CH(2)-O(1)-CH_2(1')-CH(2')=CH_2(3')} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{O(1)=CH(2)-CH_2(3)-CH_2(3')-CH(2')=CH_2(1')}$

This yields a $\gamma,\delta$-unsaturated aldehyde.

The final product is 4-pentenal, which has the structure $\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH_2-CH_2-CHO}$.

Comparing this with the given options, it matches option (B).

Answer: $\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CHO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product for the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed hydration of an alkene.

The reaction proceeds via an electrophilic addition mechanism following Markovnikov's rule.

Protonation of the exocyclic double bond in methylenecyclopentane occurs at the terminal $CH_2$ group to form the more stable tertiary carbocation (1-methylcyclopentyl cation).

Nucleophilic attack by water on this tertiary carbocation, followed by deprotonation, yields 1-methylcyclopentanol as the major product.

Option (C) represents 1-methylcyclopentanol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents can be used to convert 1-isopropylcyclopentene to 1-isopropylcyclopentanol?
options: $\mathrm{HBr}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Pd}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$
$\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, neutral
questions.solution:
The conversion of 1-isopropylcyclopentene to 1-isopropylcyclopentanol involves the addition of water across the carbon-carbon double bond.

This is an electrophilic addition reaction that follows Markovnikov's rule. The protonation of the double bond occurs to form the most stable carbocation, which in this case is a tertiary carbocation at the C1 position (bearing the isopropyl group).

Water then attacks this tertiary carbocation to form 1-isopropylcyclopentanol.

The reagent required for this acid-catalyzed hydration is water in the presence of a dilute acid, such as dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$.

$\mathrm{HBr}$ would yield an alkyl bromide, $\mathrm{H}_{2} / \mathrm{Pd}$ would reduce the alkene to an alkane, and neutral $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$ does not react with alkenes.

Answer: $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is formed as a major product when the unsymmetrical ether shown below is treated with an excess of $\mathrm{HI}$?
options: $\mathrm{ICH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2(p\text{-}\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{NO}_2)$
$\mathrm{PhI}$
$\mathrm{PhCH}_2\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{HOCH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2(p\text{-}\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{NO}_2)$
questions.solution:
The reaction of an alkyl aryl ether with $\mathrm{HI}$ proceeds via the protonation of the ether oxygen followed by nucleophilic attack by the iodide ion.

Due to the partial double bond character of the $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond between the phenyl ring and the oxygen atom (arising from resonance), this bond is stronger and difficult to break. Furthermore, nucleophilic substitution ($\mathrm{S_N2}$) does not occur at an $\mathrm{sp^2}$ hybridized aromatic carbon.

Therefore, the iodide ion ($\mathrm{I^-}$) attacks the $\mathrm{sp^3}$ hybridized carbon of the alkyl group, breaking the $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_2$ bond.

This cleavage results in the formation of phenol ($\mathrm{PhOH}$) and the corresponding alkyl iodide, $\mathrm{ICH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2(p\text{-}\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{NO}_2)$.

Phenol does not undergo further reaction with excess $\mathrm{HI}$ to form iodobenzene. Hence, the major products are phenol and $\mathrm{ICH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2(p\text{-}\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{NO}_2)$.

Answer: $\mathrm{ICH}_2\mathrm{CH}_2(p\text{-}\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_4\mathrm{NO}_2)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ethers does not form peroxides upon prolonged exposure to air?
questions.solution:
Ethers react with atmospheric oxygen in the presence of light to form peroxides. This process is known as auto-oxidation and proceeds via a free radical mechanism.

The first step of auto-oxidation involves the abstraction of an $\alpha$-hydrogen atom (a hydrogen atom attached to the carbon directly bonded to the ether oxygen) to form a free radical. Therefore, the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen is essential for an ether to form peroxides.

Let us analyze the given options:
(A) Allyl phenyl ether: The carbon adjacent to the oxygen in the allyl group ($-CH_2-$) has two $\alpha$-hydrogens.
(B) Tetrahydrofuran (THF): The carbons adjacent to the oxygen in the ring have $\alpha$-hydrogens.
(C) Diphenyl ether: The carbons adjacent to the oxygen are part of the benzene rings. These carbons are $sp^2$ hybridized and do not have any hydrogen atoms attached to them. Thus, it lacks $\alpha$-hydrogens.
(D) Diisopropyl ether: The carbons adjacent to the oxygen ($-CH-$) have one $\alpha$-hydrogen each.

Since diphenyl ether does not have any $\alpha$-hydrogens, it cannot undergo auto-oxidation and does not form peroxides upon prolonged exposure to air.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following reaction sequences can be used to achieve the transformation shown below?



Reaction Sequences:
options: Sequences 2 and 3
Sequences 1 and 2
Sequences 1 and 3
Sequences 1, 2 and 3
questions.solution:
The transformation requires converting benzyl alcohol ($C_6H_5CH_2OH$) to benzylamine ($C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$) without changing the number of carbon atoms.

In Sequence 1:
$C_6H_5CH_2OH \xrightarrow{PBr_3} C_6H_5CH_2Br \xrightarrow{N_3^-} C_6H_5CH_2N_3 \xrightarrow[H_2O]{LiAlH_4} C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$
This yields the correct product.

In Sequence 2:
$C_6H_5CH_2OH \xrightarrow{PCC} C_6H_5CHO \xrightarrow{NH_3} C_6H_5CH=NH \xrightarrow{H_2, Pd/C} C_6H_5CH_2NH_2$
This yields the correct product.

In Sequence 3:
$C_6H_5CH_2OH \xrightarrow{PBr_3} C_6H_5CH_2Br \xrightarrow{CN^-} C_6H_5CH_2CN \xrightarrow[H_2O]{LiAlH_4} C_6H_5CH_2CH_2NH_2$
This yields 2-phenylethanamine, which has an extra carbon atom.

Therefore, only Sequences 1 and 2 can achieve the desired transformation.

Answer: Sequences 1 and 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence proceeds as follows:

First, the primary alcohol ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) reacts with p-toluenesulfonyl chloride ($\text{TsCl}$) to form a tosylate ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OTs}$), converting the hydroxyl group into a good leaving group. The lactone ring remains intact during this step.

Next, the addition of potassium carbonate ($\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3$) in methanol ($\text{MeOH}$) generates methoxide ions ($\text{CH}_3\text{O}^-$). The methoxide ion attacks the carbonyl carbon of the lactone, causing the ring to open. This process yields an acyclic intermediate with a methyl ester at one end and an alkoxide ion at the $\gamma$-position.

Finally, this intermediate possesses an alkoxide ion and a tosylate group on adjacent carbons. An intramolecular $S_N2$ reaction takes place, where the alkoxide oxygen attacks the carbon bearing the tosylate group. The displacement of the tosylate leaving group results in the formation of a three-membered epoxide (oxirane) ring.

Since the stereocenter is not directly involved in the bond-breaking or forming steps of the substitution, its configuration is retained. The final product is an epoxide with a methyl ester side chain, matching the structure shown in the first option.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the appropriate reagent for the following chemical transformation:
options: NaCl
$\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$
$\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$
HCl
questions.solution:
The given reaction is a nucleophilic substitution where the hydroxyl group ($-OH$) of an alcohol is replaced by a chloride ion ($-Cl$) to form an alkyl chloride and water ($H_2O$).

Evaluating the given options:

NaCl: Cannot substitute the $-OH$ group directly because hydroxide ($-OH^-$) is a poor leaving group.

$Cl_2$: Does not react with alcohols to form alkyl chlorides; it is typically used for the halogenation of alkanes or alkenes.

$Na_2Cr_2O_7$: A strong oxidizing agent that would oxidize the given secondary alcohol to a ketone.

HCl: Provides an acidic medium to protonate the $-OH$ group, converting it into a good leaving group ($-OH_2^+$). The nucleophilic $Cl^-$ ion then attacks to form the alkyl chloride, releasing $H_2O$ as a byproduct. This perfectly matches the given chemical equation.

Thus, HCl is the appropriate reagent for this transformation.

Answer: HCl

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following chemical transformations is incorrectly depicted?
questions.solution:
Option (A): $\text{NaBH}_4$ is a mild reducing agent. It can reduce aldehydes, ketones, and acid chlorides, but it is not strong enough to reduce carboxylic acids, esters, or amides. Therefore, the reduction of cyclohex-3-ene-1-carboxylic acid to cyclohex-3-en-1-ylmethanol using $\text{NaBH}_4$ will not take place. This transformation is incorrectly depicted.

Option (B): $\text{LiAlH}_4$ is a strong reducing agent that successfully reduces carboxylic acids to primary alcohols without affecting isolated carbon-carbon double bonds. This transformation is correctly depicted.

Option (C): Thionyl chloride ($\text{SOCl}_2$) reacts with carboxylic acids to form acid chlorides, releasing $\text{SO}_2$ and $\text{HCl}$ gases. This transformation is correctly depicted.

Option (D): Grignard reagents undergo nucleophilic addition with carbon dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$) to form carboxylate salts, which upon acidic workup ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$) yield carboxylic acids. This transformation is correctly depicted.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 3,3-diphenyloxirane-2-carboxylic acid, which is a glycidic acid.

Heating a glycidic acid results in thermal decarboxylation to form an aldehyde or a ketone. This is a characteristic reaction known as the Darzens glycidic ester condensation's final step.

The mechanism involves the opening of the epoxide ring to form a stable carbocation. The C-O bond breaks such that the positive charge develops on the carbon bearing the two phenyl groups, as it is highly resonance-stabilized (benzhydryl-type cation).

This ring opening, accompanied by an intramolecular proton transfer, forms the zwitterionic intermediate $Ph_2C^{+} - CH(OH) - COO^{-}$.

Decarboxylation then occurs with the loss of $CO_2$. The electrons from the breaking C-C bond shift to form a carbon-carbon double bond, neutralizing the carbocation and yielding an enol intermediate: $Ph_2C=CH(OH)$.

Finally, the unstable enol rapidly tautomerizes to the more stable carbonyl compound, giving the major product diphenylacetaldehyde ($Ph_2CH-CHO$).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 1-(2-hydroxyphenyl)-3-phenylpropane-1,3-dione, which contains a 1,3-diketone moiety and an ortho-phenolic $-OH$ group.

In the presence of an acidic medium ($H^+$), the reaction proceeds via the following steps:
1. Protonation: The carbonyl oxygen adjacent to the phenyl ring ($-CO-Ph$) gets protonated, making its carbonyl carbon highly electrophilic.
2. Intramolecular Cyclization: The nucleophilic oxygen atom of the phenolic $-OH$ group attacks this electrophilic carbon. This intramolecular nucleophilic addition forms a six-membered cyclic hemiketal intermediate (2-hydroxy-2-phenylchroman-4-one).
3. Dehydration: The intermediate undergoes acid-catalyzed dehydration (loss of a water molecule) to form a double bond between $C_2$ and $C_3$. This results in a highly stable, fully conjugated bicyclic system.

The final major product is 2-phenyl-4H-chromen-4-one, commonly known as flavone. This matches the structure given in Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is benzene oxide (7-oxabicyclo[4.1.0]hepta-2,4-diene), which contains a single oxygen atom.

In the presence of concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the epoxide undergoes an acid-catalyzed rearrangement. The mechanism proceeds as follows:
1. The oxygen atom of the epoxide is protonated by the acid.
2. The epoxide ring opens to form a resonance-stabilized carbocation (a cyclohexadienyl cation).
3. A 1,2-hydride shift (NIH shift) occurs, leading to the formation of a protonated cyclohexadienone intermediate.
4. Loss of a proton gives cyclohexadienone, which rapidly tautomerizes to form phenol in order to gain aromatic stability.

Since the reactant has only one oxygen atom, the product must also have one oxygen atom (as no oxidizing agent is present to add another oxygen). Options (B), (C), and (D) all contain two oxygen atoms, which makes them incorrect.

Thus, the major product formed is phenol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following three proposed syntheses for phenyl propyl ether:
(i)
(ii)
(iii)
Which of the above syntheses is/are viable?
options: (i) and (iii) only
(ii) and (iii) only
(i) only
(iii) only
questions.solution:
Synthesis (i) involves the deprotonation of phenol by $\text{KNH}_2$ to form potassium phenoxide. The phenoxide ion acts as a good nucleophile and reacts with 1-bromopropane (a primary alkyl halide) via an $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ mechanism to successfully form phenyl propyl ether. This is a standard and viable Williamson ether synthesis.

Synthesis (ii) attempts to form sodium propoxide using $\text{NaOH}$, but the equilibrium does not favor alkoxide formation. More importantly, the second step requires the nucleophilic substitution of bromobenzene. Aryl halides do not undergo $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ reactions due to the partial double bond character of the $\text{C}-\text{Br}$ bond (arising from resonance) and the instability of the phenyl cation, making this route not viable.

Synthesis (iii) successfully forms sodium propoxide using the strong base $\text{NaH}$. However, the subsequent step again involves an $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ attack on bromobenzene, which is unreactive towards nucleophilic substitution under these conditions. Thus, this synthesis is also not viable.

Therefore, only synthesis (i) is viable.

Answer: (i) only

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence.
questions.solution:
The reaction sequence involves a Payne rearrangement followed by an epoxide ring opening.

1. Payne Rearrangement: The starting material is an epoxy alcohol. In the presence of a base ($\text{NaOH}$), the primary hydroxyl group is deprotonated to form an alkoxide. This alkoxide undergoes an intramolecular $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ attack on the adjacent epoxide carbon (C2), opening the original internal epoxide and forming a new, more reactive terminal epoxide.
Because this is an $\text{S}_{\text{N}}2$ process, the stereocenter at C2 undergoes inversion of configuration. The stereocenter at C3, which holds the leaving oxygen, retains its configuration. Visually, the attacking alkoxide must approach from the front (wedged) since the original epoxide oxygen is in the back (dashed). This results in the new epoxide oxygen being wedged, and the C3 oxygen becoming a dashed hydroxyl group.

2. Epoxide Ring Opening: In the second step, sodium cyanide ($\text{NaCN}$) in DMSO provides the cyanide ion ($\text{CN}^{-}$), which is a strong nucleophile. It attacks the newly formed terminal epoxide at the less sterically hindered primary carbon (C1).
This regioselective attack opens the terminal epoxide, leaving the oxygen at C2 as a hydroxyl group. Since the attack occurs at C1, the stereocenter at C2 is not involved in the bond-breaking/forming process and thus retains its inverted configuration from the first step.

Tracing the stereochemistry:
- Starting material: C2 is $(S)$, C3 is $(R)$.
- After Payne rearrangement: C2 inverts to $(R)$, C3 retains $(R)$. The intermediate is a $(2R, 3R)$-terminal epoxide.
- After $\text{CN}^{-}$ attack: C2 retains $(R)$, C3 retains $(R)$. The final product is a $(2R, 3R)$-diol.

Looking at the given options, Option (A) correctly depicts the $(2R, 3R)$ configuration with the $-\text{OH}$ at C3 being dashed (retained from the original epoxide) and the $-\text{OH}$ at C2 being wedged (resulting from the inversion during the Payne rearrangement).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a bis-epoxide with two cyclopentane rings connected by a single bond. Let's number the carbons of the epoxide rings as C1, C2 and C1', C2', with the rings connected at C3 and C3'.

Step 1: The Grignard reagent, $\text{MeMgCl}$, provides a methyl nucleophile ($\text{Me}^{-}$). Since only 1 equivalent is used, it will attack only one of the epoxide rings. Nucleophilic attack on an epoxide occurs at the less sterically hindered carbon. C1 and C1' are less hindered than C2 and C2' (which are adjacent to the C3-C3' connecting bond). Thus, $\text{Me}^{-}$ attacks C1', opening the epoxide and forming an alkoxide intermediate at C2'.

Step 2: The newly formed alkoxide at C2' acts as an internal nucleophile and attacks the second epoxide ring. This intramolecular attack can occur at C2 to form a 5-membered ring (5-exo-tet cyclization) or at C1 to form a 6-membered ring (6-endo-tet cyclization). According to Baldwin's rules, the 5-exo-tet process is kinetically favored.

Step 3: The attack of the alkoxide at C2 opens the second epoxide, forming a central 5-membered ether ring and leaving a new alkoxide at C1.

Step 4: Aqueous workup ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$) protonates the alkoxide at C1, converting it into a hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$).

The final major product is a tricyclic compound featuring a methyl group at C1', a hydroxyl group at C1, and a central tetrahydrofuran ring connecting the two cyclopentane units. This structure perfectly matches Option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction proceeds via a tandem alkylation-cyclization sequence.

First, the strong base $\text{NaOEt}$ abstracts the highly acidic proton from the $-\text{CH}_2-$ group located between the electron-withdrawing pyridine ring and the ester group, forming a resonance-stabilized carbanion.

The reagent 2-(2-bromoethyl)oxirane has two electrophilic sites: a primary alkyl bromide and an epoxide. The soft carbanion preferentially attacks the less sterically hindered and softer primary bromide via an $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ mechanism, displacing the bromide ion.

This alkylation yields the intermediate ethyl 2-(3-cyanopyridin-4-yl)-4-(oxiran-2-yl)butanoate.

A second deprotonation occurs at the same $\alpha$-carbon (which remains acidic) to generate a new carbanion.

This carbanion then undergoes an intramolecular 5-exo-tet cyclization by attacking the less hindered terminal carbon of the epoxide ring. This pathway is kinetically favored over forming a 4-membered ring.

The opening of the epoxide ring forms an alkoxide, which is subsequently protonated to yield a hydroxyl group.

The resulting major product is a cyclopentane ring with the 3-cyanopyridin-4-yl and ester groups at the 1-position, and a hydroxyl group at the 3-position. This matches the structure given in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{C}$ in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given reaction sequence involves three main steps:

1. Hydroboration-Oxidation: The reaction of 1-methylcyclopentene with $\text{BH}_3/\text{THF}$ followed by $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2/\text{NaOH}$ is a hydroboration-oxidation process. It results in the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond. The addition is strictly *syn*, meaning the hydrogen atom and the hydroxyl ($\text{-OH}$) group add to the same face of the double bond. Consequently, the methyl group at C1 and the newly added $\text{-OH}$ group at C2 will be on opposite faces of the ring, yielding trans-2-methylcyclopentanol (Product A). If we assume the methyl group is wedge (up), the $\text{-OH}$ group will be dash (down).

2. Tosylation: The reaction of Product A with $p$-toluenesulfonyl chloride ($\text{TsCl}$) in the presence of pyridine converts the alcohol into a tosylate ($\text{-OTs}$), which is an excellent leaving group. This step proceeds with *retention of configuration* at the chiral center. Thus, the $\text{-OTs}$ group remains on the same face as the original $\text{-OH}$ group. Product B is trans-2-methylcyclopentyl tosylate (methyl is wedge, $\text{-OTs}$ is dash).

3. Nucleophilic Substitution ($S_N2$): The reaction of Product B with $\text{NaCN}$ in a polar aprotic solvent like DMF favors an $S_N2$ mechanism. The cyanide nucleophile ($\text{CN}^-$) attacks the C2 carbon from the side opposite to the leaving group ($\text{-OTs}$), leading to an *inversion of configuration* at C2. Since the $\text{-OTs}$ group was dash (down), the incoming $\text{-CN}$ group will be wedge (up). The configuration at C1 remains unaffected (methyl is wedge).

As a result, both the methyl group and the cyanide group in the final product are on the same face of the ring, yielding cis-2-methylcyclopentanecarbonitrile (Product C). This corresponds to the structure where both groups are shown with wedge bonds.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The reaction involves an intramolecular nucleophilic attack by a thiolate anion on an epoxide ring.

First, sodium hydride (NaH) acts as a strong base and deprotonates the thiol (-SH) group to form a highly nucleophilic thiolate anion (-S^-).

The molecule has a chain of four carbon atoms between the sulfur atom and the epoxide ring. The epoxide is a cis-epoxide, with the alkyl chain and the methyl group on the same side (both wedged in a standard representation).

The thiolate anion can attack either carbon of the epoxide ring:
1. Attack at the carbon attached to the alkyl chain (C_$\alpha$) forms a 6-membered ring (6-exo-tet process).
2. Attack at the carbon attached to the methyl group (C_$\beta$) forms a 7-membered ring (7-endo-tet process).

According to Baldwin's rules and general kinetic preferences for ring formation, the formation of a 6-membered ring is significantly faster and more favorable than a 7-membered ring. Therefore, the thiolate will attack C_$\alpha$.

Nucleophilic opening of an epoxide proceeds via an S_N2 mechanism, which results in the inversion of stereochemistry at the attacked carbon.
Let's determine the absolute configuration of the starting epoxide:
- At C_$\alpha$: The priorities are -O (1), -C_$\beta$ (2), -alkyl chain (3), -H (4). With the chain wedged and H dashed, the configuration is (R).
- At C_$\beta$: The priorities are -O (1), -C_$\alpha$ (2), -CH_3 (3), -H (4). With the methyl wedged and H dashed, the configuration is (S).
The starting material is a (2R, 3S)-epoxide.

Upon attack at C_$\alpha$, the stereochemistry at C_$\alpha$ inverts from (R) to (S). The stereochemistry at C_$\beta$ remains unchanged as (S).
Thus, the product will have an (S, S) configuration at the two relevant stereocenters.

Looking at the given options:
- Options (B) and (C) show 7-membered rings, which are kinetically disfavored.
- Options (A) and (D) show the expected 6-membered ring (tetrahydrothiopyran derivative) with a -CH(OH)CH_3 substituent.
- In Option (A), the stereocenter on the ring has the substituent wedged, corresponding to the (S) configuration. The stereocenter on the substituent also has the -OH group wedged, corresponding to the (S) configuration. This matches our predicted (S, S) product.
- In Option (D), the ring stereocenter has the substituent dashed, corresponding to the (R) configuration, which would imply retention of stereochemistry (incorrect for S_N2).

Therefore, the major product is the one shown in Option (A).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the most suitable reagent for the following conversion:
options: $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$
$\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2} / \mathrm{HCl}$
$\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$
$\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$
questions.solution:
From the given reaction, we can observe that the $-\mathrm{OH}$ group is replaced by a $-\mathrm{Cl}$ atom with retention of configuration (both the $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ and the incoming $-\mathrm{Cl}$ are on solid wedges, meaning they are cis to each other, just like in the reactant).

Let us analyze the given reagents:
1. $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$: In the absence of a base like pyridine, the reaction of an alcohol with thionyl chloride proceeds via the $S_N i$ (internal nucleophilic substitution) mechanism. This mechanism involves the formation of a chlorosulfite ester intermediate, which decomposes to give the alkyl chloride with retention of configuration.
2. $\mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}/\mathrm{HCl}$ (Lucas reagent): This reaction proceeds via an $S_N 1$ mechanism involving a carbocation intermediate, which would lead to racemization and likely rearrangement (due to the adjacent tertiary carbon).
3. $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ and $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$: These reagents typically react with alcohols via an $S_N 2$ mechanism, which results in inversion of configuration.

Since the conversion requires retention of configuration, $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$ is the most suitable reagent.

Answer: $\mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is a stable hydrate?
options: All of these
questions.solution:
Generally, gem-diols (hydrates) are unstable and readily lose a water molecule to form the corresponding carbonyl compounds. However, they can be exceptionally stable under certain conditions:

1. Presence of strong electron-withdrawing groups: In chloral hydrate (Option B), the strongly electron-withdrawing $-CCl_3$ group increases the electrophilicity of the carbonyl carbon, favoring hydration. The hydrate is further stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the $-OH$ groups and the chlorine atoms. Similarly, in ninhydrin (Option A), the two adjacent carbonyl groups exert a strong $-I$ effect, which stabilizes the central gem-diol form.

2. Relief of angle strain: In cyclopropanone (Option D), the carbonyl carbon is $sp^2$ hybridized, which ideally requires a bond angle of $120^{\circ}$. However, the three-membered ring constrains the angle to approximately $60^{\circ}$, causing severe angle strain. Upon hydration, the carbon becomes $sp^3$ hybridized (ideal angle $109.5^{\circ}$), which significantly reduces the angle strain, making cyclopropanone hydrate highly stable.

Since all the given compounds are stable hydrates, the correct choice is 'All of these'.

Answer: All of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given substrate is an allylic ether with a bulky isopropyl group at the C4 position (wedged) and an -OR leaving group at the C3 position (dashed). The double bond is located between C1 and C2.

When this compound reacts with a secondary amine ($\ddot{N}R_2$), it undergoes nucleophilic substitution. Because it is an allylic system, the reaction can proceed via direct substitution ($S_N2$) at C3 or allylic rearrangement ($S_N2'$) at C1.

1. Regioselectivity: The C3 position is highly sterically hindered due to the adjacent bulky isopropyl group at C4. Therefore, the nucleophile preferentially attacks the less hindered $\gamma$-carbon (C1). This results in an $S_N2'$ reaction, where the double bond shifts to the C2=C3 position and the leaving group at C3 is expelled.

2. Stereoselectivity: In cyclohexenyl systems, $S_N2'$ reactions typically proceed with *syn* stereochemistry, meaning the nucleophile attacks from the same face as the departing leaving group. Since the -OR group is on a dashed bond (pointing down), the incoming $NR_2$ nucleophile will also attack from the dashed face.

Additionally, from a steric perspective, attack from the bottom (dashed) face is highly favored because it is *anti* to the bulky isopropyl group (which is wedged/pointing up), avoiding steric clash.

Thus, the major product will have the double bond between C2 and C3, and the $NR_2$ group at C1 on a dashed bond. This perfectly matches the structure given in Option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{SH} \xrightarrow[\text{(iii) } \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}]{\text{(i) Dil. } \mathrm{OH}^{-}, \text{ (ii) Ethylene oxide}} \text{Product}$
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}^{-}$
questions.solution:
In the first step, ethanethiol ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{SH}$) reacts with dilute $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ to form the ethanethiolate anion, as thiols are acidic enough to be deprotonated by hydroxide ions.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{SH} + \mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{S}^{-} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
