questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the given compound is heated, it undergoes a rearrangement to form an isomer. Identify the major product.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 3-vinyloxycyclohexene, which is an allyl vinyl ether.

When an allyl vinyl ether is heated, it undergoes a Claisen rearrangement, which is a thermally allowed [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement.

In this rearrangement, the following concerted changes occur:
1. The C-O bond between the oxygen and the allylic carbon of the cyclohexene ring breaks.
2. A new C-C bond forms between the terminal carbon of the vinyl group and the $\gamma$-carbon of the allylic system (the other end of the ring's double bond).
3. The $\pi$ electrons shift, moving the double bond in the cyclohexene ring to the adjacent position.
4. The vinyl ether part ($-O-CH=CH_2$) tautomerizes/shifts to form an aldehyde group ($-CH_2-CHO$).

The resulting product is a $\gamma,\delta$-unsaturated aldehyde. Specifically, the terminal carbon of the vinyl group attaches to the ring, and the oxygen becomes part of a carbonyl group, yielding 2-(cyclohex-2-en-1-yl)acetaldehyde.

Comparing this structure with the given options, Option (A) correctly represents this $\gamma,\delta$-unsaturated aldehyde.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a cis-1,2-diol (specifically, cis-1,2,3,4-tetrahydronaphthalene-1,2-diol).

When a cis-1,2-diol reacts with a ketone like acetone in the presence of an acid catalyst ($H^+$), it undergoes a condensation reaction to form a cyclic acetal (specifically, a ketal known as an acetonide).

The reaction proceeds via the elimination of a water molecule. The two hydroxyl oxygen atoms of the diol bond to the carbonyl carbon of acetone.

Since acetone has two methyl groups attached to its carbonyl carbon, the resulting cyclic structure will have a bridging carbon atom bonded to two methyl groups.

Looking at the given options:
Option (A) shows a hemiacetal-like structure with only one methyl group, which is incorrect.
Option (B) shows a cyclic carbonate, which would require a different reagent (like phosgene).
Option (C) correctly shows the cyclic acetonide with two methyl groups attached to the ketal carbon, preserving the cis-stereochemistry of the oxygen linkages.
Option (D) shows a structure with two hydroxyl groups on the bridging carbon, which is incorrect.

Therefore, the major product is the acetonide shown in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction sequence, given that water is continuously removed during the second step to drive the equilibrium forward:
questions.solution:
In the first step, 1-phenylethanol, which is a secondary alcohol, undergoes oxidation in the presence of Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC). PCC is a mild oxidizing agent that oxidizes secondary alcohols to ketones. Thus, 1-phenylethanol is oxidized to acetophenone.

$\text{Ph-CH(OH)-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{PCC}} \text{Ph-CO-CH}_3$

In the second step, acetophenone reacts with ethanol ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) in the presence of an acid catalyst ($\text{H}^+$). Ketones react with alcohols under acidic conditions to form ketals. The reaction proceeds via a hemiketal intermediate to form the final ketal. The continuous removal of water according to Le Chatelier's principle drives the equilibrium forward to maximize the yield of the ketal.

$\text{Ph-CO-CH}_3 + 2\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+} \text{Ph-C(OCH}_2\text{CH}_3)_2\text{-CH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$

The final major product is 1,1-diethoxy-1-phenylethane, which matches the structure in option (D).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major products formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acid-catalyzed rearrangement of a hydroperoxide, known as the Hock rearrangement (similar to the cumene hydroperoxide rearrangement).

The mechanism involves the protonation of the hydroperoxide $-\text{OH}$ group, followed by the loss of a water molecule. This creates an electron-deficient oxygen atom, prompting the migration of one of the aryl groups from the adjacent carbon to the oxygen atom.

The migratory aptitude of aryl groups depends on their electron-donating ability, as electron-donating groups better stabilize the developing positive charge in the transition state. The order of electron-donating ability for the given groups is:
$p$-tolyl ($+\text{I}$ and hyperconjugation) $>$ phenyl $>$ $p$-chlorophenyl ($-\text{I}$ effect dominates).

Therefore, the $p$-tolyl group has the highest migratory aptitude and will migrate to the oxygen atom.

After migration, the intermediate carbocation is attacked by water, forming a hemiacetal-like intermediate which then cleaves to give a phenol and a ketone.

Since the $p$-tolyl group migrates, it becomes the phenolic part, yielding $p$-cresol ($p$-methylphenol).
The remaining two groups (phenyl and $p$-chlorophenyl) stay attached to the central carbon, which becomes the carbonyl carbon, yielding $p$-chlorobenzophenone.

Thus, the major products are $p$-cresol and $p$-chlorobenzophenone, which corresponds to option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify A and B in the following reactions:
$ \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}} \mathrm{A} $
$ \left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{H}^{+}} \mathrm{B} $
options: Both A and B are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ (no reaction)
A is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ (no reaction), while B is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$
Both A and B are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO} + \mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
Both A and B are $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
questions.solution:
Tertiary alcohols are resistant to oxidation in neutral or alkaline media. Therefore, when $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ is treated with $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$, no reaction occurs and the alcohol remains unreacted. Thus, A is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$.

In acidic media, tertiary alcohols undergo dehydration to form alkenes. $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ in the presence of $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4} / \mathrm{H}^{+}$ first dehydrates to give isobutylene, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$. This alkene can further undergo oxidative cleavage by $\mathrm{KMnO}_{4}$ to yield acetone, $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$, and carbon dioxide. Thus, B is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$.

Answer: A is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{COH}$ (no reaction), while B is $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{CO}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major products $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ in the following oxidation reactions:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ and respectively
and respectively
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ and respectively
No reaction and respectively
questions.solution:
Ethers undergo auto-oxidation in the presence of light and oxygen to form hydroperoxides. The reaction takes place at the $\alpha$-carbon atom because the intermediate free radical is stabilized by the adjacent oxygen atom. Thus, diethyl ether forms 1-ethoxyethyl hydroperoxide:

$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2-O-CH_2CH_3} + \mathrm{O_2} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{h\nu}} \mathrm{CH_3CH(OOH)-O-CH_2CH_3}$ (Product A)

Cumene (isopropylbenzene) undergoes oxidation with oxygen at elevated temperatures to form cumene hydroperoxide. The oxidation occurs at the benzylic position because the resulting tertiary benzylic radical is highly stable:

$\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(CH_3)_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \xrightarrow{95-135^\circ\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{C_6H_5-C(CH_3)_2-O-OH}$ (Product B)

Comparing these products with the given options, both structures match the ones provided in option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reagents is typically used to cleave an ethereal linkage (C-O-C)?
options: Neither $\mathrm{HBr}$ nor $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
Both $\mathrm{HBr}$ and $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
$\mathrm{HBr}$
$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$
questions.solution:
Ethers are generally unreactive towards bases, oxidizing agents, and reducing agents. They undergo cleavage of the C-O-C bond only under vigorous conditions with excess of strong acids.

The cleavage of ethers is typically carried out using hydrohalic acids such as concentrated $\mathrm{HI}$ or $\mathrm{HBr}$ at high temperatures. The reaction proceeds via the protonation of the ether oxygen, followed by the nucleophilic attack of the halide ion ($\mathrm{I}^{-}$ or $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$) on the alkyl group, resulting in the formation of an alkyl halide and an alcohol.

$\mathrm{R-O-R'} + \mathrm{HBr} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{R-Br} + \mathrm{R'-OH}$

$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is a strong oxidizing agent and is not typically used for the cleavage of ethereal linkages. Therefore, $\mathrm{HBr}$ is the correct reagent.

Answer: $\mathrm{HBr}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct order of increasing acidity for the following compounds.
options: <
questions.solution:
The acidity of a compound is directly proportional to the stability of its conjugate base.

For cyclohexanol, the conjugate base (cyclohexanoxide ion) is not resonance stabilized and is destabilized by the $+I$ effect of the cyclohexyl ring.

For phenol, the conjugate base (phenoxide ion) is stabilized by resonance delocalization of the negative charge over the benzene ring, making it more acidic than cyclohexanol.

For p-nitrophenol, the conjugate base is further stabilized by the strong electron-withdrawing $-R$ and $-I$ effects of the $-NO_2$ group at the para position, making it the most acidic.

Therefore, the correct order of increasing acidity is: Cyclohexanol $<$ Phenol $<$ p-Nitrophenol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Anisole is treated with $\mathrm{HI}$ under two different conditions to yield products $\mathrm{A}$, $\mathrm{B}$, $\mathrm{C}$, and $\mathrm{D}$ as shown below:
$\mathrm{C} + \mathrm{D} \xleftarrow{\mathrm{HI}(\mathrm{g})} \mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OCH}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. } \mathrm{HI}} \mathrm{A} + \mathrm{B}$
Identify the products $\mathrm{A}$, $\mathrm{B}$, $\mathrm{C}$, and $\mathrm{D}$.
options: $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$, while there is no reaction in the second case.

$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$, while $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$ are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{I}$.
Both sets of products ($\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, as well as $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$) are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$.

$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{I}$, while $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$ are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$.
questions.solution:
The reaction of anisole ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OCH}_{3}$) with $\mathrm{HI}$ proceeds via the protonation of the ether oxygen to form an oxonium ion, $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{O}^{+}(\mathrm{H})-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$.

The $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}$ bond has partial double bond character due to the resonance between the lone pair of electrons on the oxygen atom and the benzene ring, making it stronger and difficult to break. In contrast, the $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ bond is a pure single bond.

Therefore, the nucleophile $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ attacks the less sterically hindered $\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ group via an $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}}2$ mechanism, cleaving the $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ bond. This yields phenol ($\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$) and methyl iodide ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$).

This mechanism and the resulting products remain the same regardless of whether concentrated aqueous $\mathrm{HI}$ or gaseous $\mathrm{HI}$ is used. Thus, both sets of products ($\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, as well as $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$) are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$.

Answer: Both sets of products ($\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$, as well as $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{D}$) are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{I}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{OH}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product $\mathrm{X}$ in the following reaction sequence:
options: Both B and C
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the hydroboration-oxidation of 1-methylcyclohexene.

The reagents $\text{B}_2\text{H}_6$ followed by $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ in an alkaline medium ($\text{OH}^-$) cause the anti-Markovnikov addition of water ($\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$) across the carbon-carbon double bond.

In 1-methylcyclohexene, the double bond is between C1 (which bears the methyl group) and C2. According to anti-Markovnikov regioselectivity, the $\text{OH}$ group attaches to the less substituted carbon, which is C2, while the $\text{H}$ atom attaches to the more substituted carbon, C1. This forms 2-methylcyclohexanol.

Hydroboration-oxidation proceeds via a syn-addition mechanism, meaning the $\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$ groups add to the same face of the double bond.

If $\text{H}$ and $\text{OH}$ add from the bottom face of the ring, the added $\text{H}$ at C1 will be pointing down, pushing the existing $\text{CH}_3$ group up. The added $\text{OH}$ at C2 will also be pointing down. This results in the $\text{CH}_3$ group (up) and the $\text{OH}$ group (down) being on opposite sides of the ring, forming trans-2-methylcyclohexanol.

Evaluating the given structures, the image corresponding to option (C) correctly shows the $\text{CH}_3$ group pointing up at C1 and the $\text{OH}$ group pointing down at C2, which represents the trans-2-methylcyclohexanol product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 1-Methylcyclopentene can be converted into 2-methylcyclopentanol by:
options: hydroboration-oxidation
epoxide formation followed by reduction with $\mathrm{LiAlH}_{4}$
oxymercuration-demercuration
acid-catalyzed hydration
questions.solution:
1-Methylcyclopentene contains a double bond between a tertiary carbon (C1, bearing the methyl group) and a secondary carbon (C2).

To convert 1-methylcyclopentene into 2-methylcyclopentanol, the hydroxyl group ($\text{-OH}$) must be added to the less substituted carbon (C2) and the hydrogen atom to the more substituted carbon (C1). This corresponds to the anti-Markovnikov addition of water across the double bond.

Hydroboration-oxidation of alkenes proceeds via anti-Markovnikov addition, yielding 2-methylcyclopentanol.

Oxymercuration-demercuration and acid-catalyzed hydration proceed via Markovnikov addition, yielding 1-methylcyclopentanol.

Epoxide formation followed by reduction with $\text{LiAlH}_{4}$ involves hydride attack at the less sterically hindered carbon (C2), leaving the hydroxyl group at the more substituted carbon (C1), which also yields 1-methylcyclopentanol.

Answer: hydroboration-oxidation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the Elbs persulfate oxidation.

When phenol is treated with potassium persulfate ($K_2S_2O_8$) in an alkaline medium ($HO^- / H_2O$), it undergoes oxidation to form a dihydric phenol.

The persulfate ion attacks the phenoxide ion primarily at the para position to form an intermediate sulfate ester. Upon hydrolysis, this intermediate yields hydroquinone (benzene-1,4-diol) as the major product. If the para position is blocked, the ortho product is formed.

Since the para position in phenol is unsubstituted, the major product is hydroquinone.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following alcohols is most reactive towards aqueous $\mathrm{HBr}$?
options: 1-Phenyl-2-propanol
3-Phenyl-1-propanol
2-Phenyl-1-propanol
1-Phenyl-1-propanol
questions.solution:
The reaction of alcohols with aqueous $\mathrm{HBr}$ proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate ($S_{N}1$ mechanism). The reactivity of the alcohol is directly proportional to the stability of the carbocation formed.

Let us analyze the carbocations formed from the given alcohols:

1-Phenyl-2-propanol forms $C_6H_5-CH_2-CH^{+}-CH_3$, which is a secondary carbocation.

3-Phenyl-1-propanol forms $C_6H_5-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2^{+}$, which is a primary carbocation.

2-Phenyl-1-propanol forms $C_6H_5-CH(CH_3)-CH_2^{+}$, which is a primary carbocation.

1-Phenyl-1-propanol forms $C_6H_5-CH^{+}-CH_2-CH_3$, which is a secondary benzylic carbocation.

The secondary benzylic carbocation is highly stabilized by resonance with the adjacent phenyl ring. Since 1-phenyl-1-propanol forms the most stable carbocation, it is the most reactive towards aqueous $\mathrm{HBr}$.

Answer: 1-Phenyl-1-propanol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reactions of tert-butyl methyl ether:

$\text{Products } (\mathrm{P}_2) \xleftarrow{\text{anhy. HI}} (\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{conc. HI}} \text{Products } (\mathrm{P}_1)$

The products $\mathrm{P}_1$ and $\mathrm{P}_2$, respectively, are:
options: $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$ and $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$
$(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ and $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$
$(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ in both cases
$(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$ in both cases
questions.solution:
The cleavage of ethers by $\mathrm{HI}$ involves the initial protonation of the oxygen atom to form an oxonium ion.

For the reaction with concentrated $\mathrm{HI}$:
Concentrated $\mathrm{HI}$ is an aqueous solution, providing a highly polar medium that stabilizes carbocation intermediates. Thus, the reaction proceeds via an $S_N1$ mechanism. The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond cleaves to form the highly stable tert-butyl carbocation, which is then attacked by the iodide ion. The products are tert-butyl iodide and methanol.
$\mathrm{P}_1$: $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$

For the reaction with anhydrous $\mathrm{HI}$:
In the absence of a polar solvent, the formation of a carbocation is not favored due to the lack of solvation energy. Therefore, the reaction proceeds via an $S_N2$ mechanism. The iodide ion attacks the less sterically hindered carbon atom, which is the methyl group. The $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ bond cleaves to yield methyl iodide and tert-butyl alcohol.
$\mathrm{P}_2$: $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$

Therefore, $\mathrm{P}_1$ and $\mathrm{P}_2$ respectively are $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ and $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$.

Answer: $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{CI} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{OH}$ and $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_3\mathrm{COH} + \mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{I}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When cyclobutylethene (vinylcyclobutane) is treated with dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, the major product formed is:
options: 2-Methylcyclopentan-1-ol
2-Cyclobutylethan-1-ol
1-Cyclobutylethan-1-ol
1-Methylcyclopentan-1-ol
questions.solution:
Protonation of the double bond in cyclobutylethene (vinylcyclobutane) by dilute $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ follows Markovnikov's rule, yielding the more stable secondary carbocation, the 1-cyclobutylethyl cation.

To relieve the significant ring strain of the four-membered cyclobutane ring, a ring expansion occurs. A $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond from the ring migrates to the adjacent carbocation center, expanding the ring to a five-membered cyclopentane ring. This forms the 2-methylcyclopentyl cation, which is still a secondary carbocation.

To achieve greater stability, a 1,2-hydride shift takes place from the tertiary carbon (bearing the methyl group) to the adjacent secondary carbocation. This rearrangement forms the highly stable 1-methylcyclopentyl cation (a tertiary carbocation).

Finally, nucleophilic attack by water on this tertiary carbocation, followed by deprotonation, yields 1-methylcyclopentan-1-ol as the major product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the correct decreasing order of acidic character for the following compounds: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$, $\mathrm{MeOH}$, $\mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH}$, $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH}$, and $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$.
options: $\mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$
$\mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH} > \mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
$\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$
questions.solution:
The acidic strength depends on the stability of the conjugate base formed after the removal of a proton.

The conjugate bases are $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{C}^{-}$, $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{O}^{-}$, $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{CHO}^{-}$, $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CO}^{-}$, and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$.

Oxygen is more electronegative than sp-hybridized carbon, so the negative charge is more stable on the oxygen atom. Therefore, water and alcohols are more acidic than propyne ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$).

Among water and alcohols, the electron-donating $+I$ effect of alkyl groups destabilizes the alkoxide ion. The $+I$ effect increases in the order: $-\mathrm{CH}_{3} < -\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2} < -\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}$. This makes secondary and tertiary alcohols less acidic than water.

Methanol ($\mathrm{MeOH}$) is slightly more acidic than water. The $pK_a$ values are approximately $15.5$ for $\mathrm{MeOH}$, $15.7$ for $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$, $16.5$ for $\mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH}$, $17.0$ for $\mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH}$, and $25$ for $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$.

Thus, the correct decreasing order of acidic character is $\mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{MeOH} > \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O} > \mathrm{Me}_{2}\mathrm{CHOH} > \mathrm{Me}_{3}\mathrm{COH} > \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{C}\equiv\mathrm{CH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (picric acid) can be prepared in good yield by:
options: both of these methods
neither of these methods
the nitration of 2,4-dinitrophenol
the nitration of 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene
questions.solution:
Direct nitration of phenol with concentrated nitric acid gives a poor yield of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid) due to extensive oxidation of the highly activated aromatic ring.

To achieve a good yield, the ring must be deactivated prior to the final nitration step. This is commonly done by first synthesizing 2,4-dinitrophenol (often from the hydrolysis of 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene). The nitration of 2,4-dinitrophenol proceeds smoothly to give picric acid in good yield because the presence of two strongly electron-withdrawing nitro groups protects the ring from oxidative cleavage by nitric acid.

In contrast, the nitration of 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene yields 2,4,6-trinitrochlorobenzene (picryl chloride), not picric acid. Therefore, only the nitration of 2,4-dinitrophenol directly yields picric acid.

Answer: the nitration of 2,4-dinitrophenol

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the following alcohol is treated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$, what is the major product obtained?
options: All three products are formed in equal amounts.
questions.solution:
The given reactant is 1-phenyl-3-methylbutan-2-ol, which has the structure $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH(CH}_3)_2$.

When treated with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, the hydroxyl group is protonated and leaves as a water molecule, generating a secondary carbocation at C-2:
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}^+\text{H}-\text{CH(CH}_3)_2$

This intermediate carbocation can undergo a 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent C-1 (benzylic position) to form a highly resonance-stabilized secondary benzylic carbocation:
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{C}^+\text{H}-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH(CH}_3)_2$

Elimination of a proton ($\text{H}^+$) from C-2 of this rearranged carbocation yields a double bond conjugated with the benzene ring:
$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH(CH}_3)_2$

Conjugation with the phenyl ring provides significant thermodynamic stability to the alkene, making it the major product over the unconjugated trisubstituted alkene (Option 4) that would form from a hydride shift from C-3.

Because of the bulky nature of the phenyl group and the isopropyl group, the sterically less hindered trans (E) isomer is overwhelmingly favored over the cis (Z) isomer. Looking at the given options:
Option (1) shows the bulky phenyl and isopropyl groups on the same side of the double bond (cis isomer).
Option (3) shows the bulky phenyl and isopropyl groups on opposite sides of the double bond (trans isomer).

Therefore, the trans isomer is the thermodynamically most stable product and thus the major product.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is the strongest reducing agent?
options: $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{OCMe}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{H}\right]^{-}$
All of the above are equally strong reducing agents
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{AlH}_{4}\right]^{-}$
$\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHMe}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{H}$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{AlH}_{4}\right]^{-}$ (Lithium aluminium hydride) is a powerful reducing agent capable of reducing a wide variety of functional groups, including carboxylic acids, esters, amides, and nitriles, to their corresponding alcohols or amines.

$\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{OCMe}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{H}\right]^{-}$ (Lithium tri-tert-butoxyaluminium hydride) is a milder reducing agent due to the steric hindrance and electron-withdrawing nature of the tert-butoxy groups. It is typically used to selectively reduce acid chlorides to aldehydes.

$\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CHMe}_{2}\right)_{2} \mathrm{H}$ (DIBAL-H) is also a milder, electrophilic reducing agent commonly used for the partial reduction of esters and nitriles to aldehydes at low temperatures.

Therefore, $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{AlH}_{4}\right]^{-}$ is the strongest reducing agent among the given options.

Answer: $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left[\mathrm{AlH}_{4}\right]^{-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the given esterification reaction:
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH} + \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3} + \mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}$
The yield of the ester can be increased by:
options: all of these
using acetic acid in excess
removing water from the reaction mixture
using ethanol in excess
questions.solution:
According to Le Chatelier's principle, the yield of the products in a reversible reaction can be increased by shifting the equilibrium in the forward direction.

For the given esterification reaction, the equilibrium can be shifted forward by:
1. Increasing the concentration of reactants, which can be done by using either ethanol or acetic acid in excess.
2. Decreasing the concentration of products, which can be done by removing water from the reaction mixture as it is formed.

Since all the given methods shift the equilibrium in the forward direction, the yield of the ester is increased by all of them.

Answer: all of these

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in decreasing order of their acidic strength:
(I) Phenol
(II) p-Nitrophenol
(III) m-Cresol
(IV) p-Cresol
options: (II) > (I) > (IV) > (III)
(II) > (I) > (III) > (IV)
(III) > (IV) > (II) > (I)
(II) > (III) > (IV) > (I)
questions.solution:
Acidic strength of phenols depends on the stability of the phenoxide ion. Electron-withdrawing groups increase acidity, while electron-donating groups decrease acidity.

In $p$-nitrophenol (II), the $-\text{NO}_2$ group exerts strong $-\text{I}$ and $-\text{M}$ effects, highly stabilizing the phenoxide ion. Thus, it is the most acidic.

Phenol (I) has no substituents, making it more acidic than the cresols which contain electron-donating methyl groups.

In $m$-cresol (III), the $-\text{CH}_3$ group exerts only a $+\text{I}$ effect (hyperconjugation is absent at the meta position), which destabilizes the phenoxide ion.

In $p$-cresol (IV), the $-\text{CH}_3$ group exerts both $+\text{I}$ and $+\text{H}$ (hyperconjugation) effects. The $+\text{H}$ effect strongly destabilizes the phenoxide ion, making it the least acidic.

Therefore, the decreasing order of acidic strength is (II) > (I) > (III) > (IV).

Answer: (II) > (I) > (III) > (IV)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following compounds in increasing order of their acidic strength:
(i) Phenol
(ii) m-Nitrophenol
(iii) m-Chlorophenol
(iv) m-Cresol
options: (iv) < (i) < (iii) < (ii)
(iii) < (ii) < (iv) < (i)
(i) < (iv) < (iii) < (ii)
(iv) < (i) < (ii) < (iii)
questions.solution:
Acidic strength of phenols increases with the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (EWG) and decreases with electron-donating groups (EDG).

At the meta position, resonance effects do not operate, so only inductive effects influence the stability of the phenoxide ion.

In $m$-cresol (iv), the $-\text{CH}_3$ group exerts a $+\text{I}$ effect, which destabilizes the phenoxide ion, making it less acidic than phenol (i).

In $m$-chlorophenol (iii), the $-\text{Cl}$ group exerts a $-\text{I}$ effect, stabilizing the phenoxide ion and making it more acidic than phenol.

In $m$-nitrophenol (ii), the $-\text{NO}_2$ group exerts a very strong $-\text{I}$ effect, which is stronger than the $-\text{I}$ effect of $-\text{Cl}$. This makes it the most acidic among the given compounds.

Thus, the increasing order of acidic strength is (iv) $<$ (i) $<$ (iii) $<$ (ii).

Answer: (iv) < (i) < (iii) < (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Compound shown below is formed by the reaction of:
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COOH}$ and
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{COCH}_{2}\mathrm{COOH}$ and
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CHO}$ and
questions.solution:
The given compound is a cyclic acetal, which is formed by the reaction between a carbonyl compound (aldehyde or ketone) and a diol.

To identify the reactants, we cleave the C-O bonds at the acetal carbon (the carbon bonded to two oxygen atoms in the ring).

The acetal carbon is bonded to a methyl group ($-CH_3$) and a hydrogen atom. This indicates that the carbonyl compound used is acetaldehyde ($CH_3CHO$).

The rest of the molecule comes from the diol. Cleaving the acetal linkages and adding hydrogen back to the oxygen atoms gives the structure of the parent diol.

Tracing the carbon chain between the two oxygen atoms gives the skeleton of the diol: one oxygen is attached to a primary $-CH_2$ group, followed by another $-CH_2$ group, which is then attached to a tertiary carbon. This tertiary carbon is bonded to a $-CH_3$ group, a $-CH_2COOH$ group, and the second oxygen atom.

Thus, the complete structure of the diol is $HO-CH_2-CH_2-C(OH)(CH_3)-CH_2COOH$.

Comparing these two reactants with the given choices, the combination of $CH_3CHO$ and the derived diol corresponds exactly to option (2).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the reagent required to carry out the following transformation:
options: $\mathrm{HOCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}$
$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$
questions.solution:
The given transformation shows the conversion of a ketone (acetophenone) into a cyclic ketal.

The formation of a cyclic ketal from a ketone requires a diol in the presence of an acid catalyst. The product shown is a 1,3-dioxolane derivative, which is a 5-membered ring containing two oxygen atoms separated by one carbon atom (the original carbonyl carbon).

This specific 5-membered cyclic ketal is formed by the reaction of the ketone with a 1,2-diol. Among the given options, ethylene glycol ($\mathrm{HOCH}_{2}CH_{2}\mathrm{OH}$) is a 1,2-diol.

The reaction proceeds via nucleophilic addition of the hydroxyl groups of ethylene glycol to the carbonyl carbon, catalyzed by acid, with the elimination of a water molecule.

Therefore, the required reagent is $\mathrm{HOCH}_{2}CH_{2}\mathrm{OH}$.

Answer: $\mathrm{HOCH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{OH}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Predict the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reaction involves the acid-catalyzed dehydration of benzocyclobutenol.

Concentrated $H_2SO_4$ with heat ($\Delta$) acts as a dehydrating agent.

The $-OH$ group is protonated by the acid to form a good leaving group, $-O^{+}H_2$.

A water molecule is eliminated, generating a benzylic carbocation on the four-membered ring.

A proton ($H^+$) is then eliminated from the adjacent methylene carbon atom in the cyclobutane ring.

This elimination results in the formation of a carbon-carbon double bond, yielding benzocyclobutadiene as the final product.

This structure corresponds to the one given in option (1).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reactant is bicyclo[3.1.0]hex-2-en-4-ol.

Treatment with concentrated $H_2SO_4$ and heat initiates dehydration. Protonation of the $-OH$ group and subsequent loss of an $H_2O$ molecule generates a carbocation at the $C_4$ position.

This carbocation is adjacent to both a double bond (allylic) and a cyclopropane ring (cyclopropylcarbinyl). Driven by the relief of significant ring strain, the intermediate undergoes a ring expansion.

The $C_1-C_5$ bond of the fused cyclopropane ring breaks, and its electrons shift to form a new $\pi$ bond between $C_4$ and $C_5$. This electrocyclic rearrangement expands the bicyclic framework into a single six-membered ring, shifting the positive charge to $C_1$.

The resulting intermediate is the cyclohexa-2,4-dienyl cation (also known as the benzenium ion), which features conjugated double bonds at $C_2-C_3$ and $C_4-C_5$.

Finally, the loss of a proton from the adjacent $C_6$ position forms a third conjugated double bond, yielding benzene, which is a highly stable aromatic compound.

Because of the strong thermodynamic driving force to form an aromatic ring, benzene is the major product.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 2-Methylpropan-2-ol can be obtained by the acid-catalyzed hydration of which of the following alkenes?
options: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$
Any of the above
$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$
questions.solution:
Target molecule: 2-methylpropan-2-ol, which has the structure $\mathrm{(CH_3)_3C-OH}$.

Acid-catalyzed hydration of alkenes proceeds via the formation of a carbocation intermediate, following Markovnikov's rule.

- Hydration of 1-butene ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$) forms a secondary carbocation, yielding 2-butanol.
- Hydration of 2-butene ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$) also forms a secondary carbocation, yielding 2-butanol.
- The structure in option (C) is 2-methylpropene ($\mathrm{(CH_3)_2C=CH_2}$). Protonation of the double bond yields a highly stable tertiary carbocation ($\mathrm{(CH_3)_3C^+}$). Nucleophilic attack by water followed by deprotonation gives 2-methylpropan-2-ol.

Therefore, 2-methylpropene is the correct alkene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following alcohols in order of increasing ease of acid-catalysed dehydration:

(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
(iii) $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
(iv) $\mathrm{F}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}\mathrm{OH}$
options: (iv) < (iii) < (i) < (ii)
(iv) < (iii) < (ii) < (i)
(ii) < (i) < (iv) < (iii)
(ii) < (i) < (iii) < (iv)
questions.solution:
The ease of acid-catalysed dehydration of alcohols is directly proportional to the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed after the protonation and loss of a water molecule.

The carbocations formed from the given alcohols are:
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$
(ii) $\mathrm{C}_{6}\mathrm{H}_{5}\mathrm{CH}_{2}^{+}$
(iii) $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}^{+}$
(iv) $\mathrm{F}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}^{+}$

The benzyl carbocation (ii) is highly stabilized by resonance with the benzene ring, making it the most stable.

The ethyl carbocation (i) is stabilized by the +I effect and hyperconjugation of the methyl group.

The carbocations (iii) and (iv) are strongly destabilized by the -I effect of the halogen atoms. Since fluorine is more electronegative than chlorine, the -I effect of the $\mathrm{CF}_{3}$ group is stronger than that of the $\mathrm{CCl}_{3}$ group. Therefore, $\mathrm{F}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}^{+}$ is less stable than $\mathrm{Cl}_{3}\mathrm{CCH}_{2}^{+}$.

The overall order of stability of the carbocations is (iv) < (iii) < (i) < (ii).

The ease of dehydration follows the same order.

Answer: (iv) < (iii) < (i) < (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The reaction involves the acid-catalyzed ring opening of an epoxide by a nitrile (a Ritter-type reaction) to form an oxazoline ring.

Step 1: The Lewis acid $\text{BF}_3$ coordinates to the epoxide oxygen, activating the ring for nucleophilic attack.

Step 2: The nucleophile, acetonitrile ($\text{MeCN}$), attacks the epoxide. Under acidic conditions, the attack occurs at the carbon atom that can better stabilize a partial positive charge in the transition state. The epoxide has two carbons: C2 is attached to an electron-withdrawing $-\text{CONH}_2$ group, and C3 is attached to an electron-donating $-\text{CH}_3$ group. The $-\text{CH}_3$ group stabilizes the positive charge, so $\text{MeCN}$ attacks at C3.

Step 3: The attack of $\text{MeCN}$ at C3 proceeds via an $\text{S}_\text{N}2$-like mechanism, resulting in the inversion of configuration at C3. The starting material is a trans-epoxide: at C3, the $-\text{CH}_3$ is dashed (pointing away), and at C2, the $-\text{CONH}_2$ is wedged (pointing towards).

Step 4: After the attack, the C3-O bond breaks. The oxygen remains attached to C2 with its original stereochemistry ($-\text{CONH}_2$ remains wedged). At C3, the incoming nitrilium group is anti to the oxygen atom.

Step 5: To form the 5-membered oxazoline ring, the intermediate must undergo a $180^\circ$ rotation around the C2-C3 bond to bring the oxygen atom and the nitrilium carbon into proximity for cyclization.

Step 6: This rotation changes the relative orientation of the substituents at C3. The $-\text{CH}_3$ group, which was originally dashed (anti to the oxygen's original position), rotates to become wedged (syn to the oxygen's new relative position).

Step 7: Following cyclization, both the $-\text{CH}_3$ group at C4 and the $-\text{CONH}_2$ group at C5 of the newly formed oxazoline ring are on the same side (cis to each other), meaning both are wedged.

This regiochemistry and stereochemistry perfectly match the structure given in Option (B).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
The given reactant is a 2,3-epoxy alcohol derivative, specifically attached to a 1,3-dioxolane ring. The side chain is $-CH(O)CH-CH_2OH$.

When a 2,3-epoxy alcohol is treated with a nucleophile (such as $PhS^-$ from $PhSNa$) in an aqueous or protic medium, it typically undergoes a Payne rearrangement. The base or the nucleophile facilitates the reversible intramolecular migration of the epoxide from the internal position to the terminal position:

R-CH(O)CH-CH_2OH $\rightleftharpoons$ R-CH(OH)-CH(O)CH_2

Although the equilibrium generally favors the more substituted internal epoxide, the newly formed terminal epoxide (1,2-epoxy-3-alcohol) is significantly less sterically hindered and much more reactive towards nucleophilic attack.

According to the Curtin-Hammett principle, the reaction proceeds via the more reactive intermediate. The thiophenoxide ion ($PhS^-$) regioselectively attacks the least hindered terminal carbon ($C_1$) of the terminal epoxide.

This nucleophilic attack opens the epoxide ring, resulting in a 1-substituted-2,3-diol.

Stereochemically, the Payne rearrangement involves an intramolecular backside attack, which inverts the stereocenter at $C_2$. The subsequent intermolecular attack by $PhS^-$ at the primary carbon ($C_1$) does not affect the stereocenter at $C_2$. Starting from a cis-epoxide, this double-inversion process (one at $C_2$, none at $C_3$) leads to an anti-diol relative stereochemistry.

Looking at the given options, only Option (B) presents the correct regiochemistry of a 1-phenylthio-2,3-diol with the expected anti-diol stereochemistry. The other options either lack the necessary hydroxyl groups or have an incorrect carbon skeleton (e.g., extra methyl groups).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction.
questions.solution:
The reaction involves a semi-pinacol rearrangement triggered by electrophilic addition.

1. Electrophilic Attack: The starting material contains an electron-rich enol ether double bond in the dihydrofuran ring. N-Bromosuccinimide (NBS) provides an electrophilic bromine source ($Br^+$). The $Br^+$ attacks the $\beta$-carbon (C3) of the enol ether to form a stable oxonium ion intermediate at the $\alpha$-carbon (C2).

2. Intermediate Formation: The intermediate formed has a positive charge at the C2 position, which is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent oxygen atom (forming an oxonium ion: $-O^+=C-$). This electrophilic center is directly adjacent to the cyclobutanol ring.

3. Semi-Pinacol Rearrangement: The presence of the oxonium ion adjacent to the strained cyclobutane ring initiates a ring expansion. One of the C-C bonds of the cyclobutane ring migrates to the C2 carbon. This migration relieves the ring strain of the four-membered ring by expanding it to a five-membered cyclopentane ring.

4. Product Formation: As the alkyl group migrates, the carbon atom of the cyclobutane ring that originally held the $-OH$ group becomes a carbonyl carbon ($C=O$) after losing a proton. The resulting structure is a spiro[4.4] system consisting of a cyclopentanone ring and a tetrahydrofuran ring.

5. Regiochemistry: The bromine atom remains attached to the carbon adjacent to the spiro carbon in the tetrahydrofuran ring. The carbonyl group is also adjacent to the spiro carbon in the newly formed cyclopentanone ring.

Comparing this derived structure with the given options, Option (B) perfectly matches the expected spirocyclic product with the correct placement of the ketone, the oxygen atom, and the bromine atom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:
questions.solution:
When 2,4,6-triaminobenzoic acid is heated with aqueous acid ($\text{H}_3\text{O}^+$, $\Delta$), it undergoes decarboxylation followed by hydrolysis.

In the acidic medium, the $-\text{NH}_2$ groups are protonated to $-\text{NH}_3^+$. These strongly electron-withdrawing groups facilitate decarboxylation upon heating, yielding 1,3,5-triaminobenzene.

Unlike typical aromatic amines, 1,3,5-triaminobenzene is susceptible to hydrolysis because it can tautomerize to an intermediate triimine (cyclohexane-1,3,5-triimine).

This triimine is readily hydrolyzed by the aqueous acid to cyclohexane-1,3,5-trione, which then tautomerizes to its stable aromatic enol form, 1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene (phloroglucinol).

Therefore, the major product is phloroglucinol.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:

Step 1: + TsCl (2 equiv.) / pyridine
Step 2: Product of Step 1 +
Step 3: Aq. NaOH
Step 4: $\mathrm{H}^{+} / \Delta$
questions.solution:
Step 1: The starting material is a derivative of D-threitol, where the two methoxy (-OMe) groups are on opposite sides in the Fischer projection. Reaction with 2 equivalents of tosyl chloride (TsCl) in pyridine selectively tosylates the two primary alcohols, forming 1,4-di-O-tosyl-2,3-di-O-methyl-D-threitol.

Step 2: Ethyl acetoacetate is deprotonated by NaOEt to form an enolate, which undergoes a double $S_N2$ substitution with the ditosylate. The enolate carbon attacks C1 and C4 of the ditosylate, forming a cyclopentane ring. Because the reaction occurs at the primary carbons, the stereocenters at C2 and C3 are unaffected. In the starting threitol derivative, the -OMe groups are anti-like (on opposite sides in the Fischer projection). When the straight chain closes to form a cyclopentane ring, these groups end up trans to each other.

Step 3 & 4: Aqueous NaOH hydrolyzes the ester group (-COOEt) of the substituted ethyl acetoacetate to a carboxylate. Subsequent acidification ($H^+$) and heating ($\Delta$) cause the resulting $\beta$-keto acid to undergo decarboxylation, removing the -COOH group as $CO_2$.

The final product is 1-acetyl-3,4-dimethoxycyclopentane. The relative stereochemistry of the two -OMe groups must be trans. Looking at the given options:
Option (A) shows a cis isomer (both -OMe groups are wedged).
Option (B) shows a cis isomer (both -OMe groups are wedged).
Option (C) shows a trans isomer (one -OMe is dashed, the other is wedged).
Option (D) shows an alkene, which is incorrect.

Therefore, the major product is the trans isomer shown in Option (C).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product of the following reaction:
questions.solution:
Protonation of the terminal alkene by dilute $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ follows Markovnikov's rule, generating a stable tertiary carbocation at the substituted carbon.

The adjacent primary hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) acts as an internal nucleophile, attacking the tertiary carbocation to form a stable 5-membered cyclic ether via intramolecular cyclization.

Deprotonation yields the neutral tetrahydrofuran derivative.

As the starting material has two such alkene and hydroxyl pairs, this cyclization process occurs twice.

The second cyclization forms another 5-membered ring sharing the central quaternary carbon, resulting in a spiro bicyclic compound.

The major product is a spiro compound with two identical oxygen-containing 5-membered rings, matching the structure in option (B).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed when the given compound is treated with concentrated $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$.
questions.solution:
The given starting material is 6-ethyl-3-ethylidene-3,4-dihydro-2H-pyran. When treated with concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, it undergoes an acid-catalyzed ring opening followed by rearrangement and intramolecular aldol condensation to form a cyclohexenone derivative.

Step 1: Protonation of the enol ether double bond at C5 generates an oxocarbenium ion at C6, which undergoes hydrolysis (with trace water present or during workup) to open the pyran ring. This yields an intermediate allylic alcohol with a ketone group:
$\text{HO-CH}_2\text{-C(=CH-CH}_3\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-C(=O)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3$

Step 2: Under strongly acidic conditions, the allylic alcohol undergoes an allylic rearrangement and a pinacol-like hydride shift.
Protonation and loss of water gives an allylic carbocation, which is attacked by water at the more substituted position to form a new allylic alcohol. Subsequent protonation of the double bond and a hydride shift (pinacol rearrangement) converts this moiety into a methyl ketone.
The resulting open-chain intermediate is a 1,5-diketone:
$\text{CH}_3\text{-C(=O)-CH(CH}_3\text{)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{-C(=O)-CH}_2\text{-CH}_3$
(3-methyloctane-2,6-dione)

Step 3: The 1,5-diketone undergoes an intramolecular aldol condensation. The enol formed at the C7 position (the $\text{-CH}_2\text{-}$ of the ethyl group) attacks the C2 carbonyl group.
This cyclization forms a 6-membered ring. Dehydration of the resulting $\beta$-hydroxy ketone yields a conjugated cyclohexenone.

Let's trace the substituents on the newly formed cyclohexenone ring:
- The nucleophilic carbon (C7) has a methyl group attached (C8).
- The attacked carbonyl carbon (C2) has a methyl group attached (C1).
- The $\alpha$-carbon to the other ketone (C3) also has a methyl group.
After dehydration, the double bond forms between the former C7 and C2 carbons. Numbering the cyclohexenone ring from the ketone carbonyl as C1, the double bond is at C2-C3. The methyl groups are located at positions 2, 3, and 4 of the cyclohexenone ring.

Thus, the final major product is 2,3,4-trimethylcyclohex-2-en-1-one, which corresponds to option (C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the major product formed in the following reaction sequence:
questions.solution:
The given starting material is fluorenone, which contains a five-membered ring with a ketone group flanked by two benzene rings.

Step (i): Reaction with diazomethane ($\text{CH}_2\text{N}_2$) leads to the homologation of the ketone (ring expansion). The nucleophilic carbon of diazomethane attacks the carbonyl carbon, followed by the migration of an aryl bond and the expulsion of nitrogen gas ($\text{N}_2$). This expands the five-membered ring to a six-membered ring, forming phenanthren-9(10H)-one.

Phenanthren-9(10H)-one readily undergoes tautomerization to its enol form, 9-phenanthrol, because the enol form gains significant stability by forming a fully conjugated, aromatic phenanthrene system.

Step (ii): Sodium hydride ($\text{NaH}$) is a strong base that deprotonates the acidic hydroxyl group of 9-phenanthrol, forming a stable phenanthrolate anion.

Step (iii): The addition of methyl iodide ($\text{MeI}$) results in a Williamson ether synthesis. The phenanthrolate anion acts as a nucleophile and attacks the methyl group via an $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ mechanism, displacing the iodide ion.

The final product is 9-methoxyphenanthrene. By simply counting the oxygen atoms, fluorenone has only one oxygen atom, so the final product must also contain exactly one oxygen atom. Option (C) is the only structure with a single oxygen atom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify the major product formed in the following reaction:

+ $\longrightarrow$ Product

(Reaction is carried out in the presence of )
questions.solution:
The given reaction is the acetylation of salicylic acid (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) using acetic anhydride in the presence of pyridine.

Pyridine acts as a base and facilitates the removal of a proton from the more acidic and more nucleophilic phenolic $-\text{OH}$ group. The phenoxide ion (or the activated phenolic oxygen) then attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetic anhydride.

The phenolic $-\text{OH}$ group undergoes acetylation to form an ester linkage, while the carboxylic acid ($-\text{COOH}$) group remains unaffected because it is less nucleophilic.

The reaction yields 2-acetoxybenzoic acid, commonly known as aspirin, along with acetic acid as a byproduct.

Salicylic acid + Acetic anhydride $\xrightarrow{\text{Pyridine}}$ Acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin) + Acetic acid

The structure of acetylsalicylic acid corresponds to the molecule shown in option (D).
